channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: The tiny moments of joy
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
The monsoons arrived early this year, more than a week ahead of the expected landfall of June 1 in Kerala, according to the Indian Meteorological Bureau. From where I sit, in Hyderabad, this has meant overcast skies at a time in May when the heat is usually touching the low forties and we are still complaining. Suddenly, it seems like the summer has gone even before we have had enough time to grumble about it! So even though we have laid our ideas about geography to rest (for now) in the pages of our May issue, it’s clear that there’s always something to say about the weather. Maybe that’s something the British had right, in terms of conversation starters!

But weak jokes aside, once you begin paying attention to the little ways in which our surroundings shift and change, when trees flower and shed leaves, when kittens suddenly show up out of nowhere (as they have, in my garden), when the moon shades orange rather than yellow, and how bird calls change timbre, there’s no end to wonder. I find that every day when I am on my long drive to work, my eyes search through all the confusion of traffic for that patch of flaming gulmohur in the distance, or the brilliant yellow of the copper pod, and, if I’m lucky, the resplendence of the amaltas before they disappear after a too-brief flowering season. People often ask me how I can handle the distance and the tedious drive. Even as I acknowledge the extreme privilege of watching the world through the comfort of a car, I also know it is because I have learned how to focus on the beauty that is in the here and now, such as is available to me. Many years ago, when I had occasion to travel in the crowded local trains of Mumbai, I remember being astonished at how people were able to ignore the grime and the sweat and the congestion and do such things as play cards, string beans, or work on the crossword puzzle. Women would smile through their tiredness, exchanging jokes and catching up on gossip, even as their blouses turned dark with sweat.

There are some things we have little control over – weather and traffic, among others – and the only way to deal with them is to not pay them attention but instead to distract ourselves (or occupy ourselves) with the things that can bring us immediate joy.

It’s easy to dismiss this as a trite homily, but most of us will recognize the truth in triteness – we dismiss it not because it is untrue, but because we’ve heard it so often. But as we all know only too well (and we see in our classrooms every day), hearing does not always mean taking in, much less acting on. Maybe it’s time to start...with the tiniest joys?
item.excerpt:encoded: The monsoons arrived early this year, more than a week ahead of the expected landfall of June 1 in Kerala, according to the Indian Meteorological Bureau. From where I sit, in Hyderabad, this has meant overcast skies at a time in May when the heat is usually touching the low forties and we are still complaining.

item.title: Peer mentoring: empathy in action
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nivedita Bedadur

I stood at the edge of the knoll, jutting out into the sea. My hair flew, my heart sank, and I thought, “This is the end. A pity, at the age of 30…”

I was 30. I had always dreamt of being a teacher. But things didn’t work out that way. A wrong turn and lack of funds meant having to work so that I could educate myself. And now that my dream had finally come true and I had my coveted position as a teacher, nothing but disappointment followed me. My seniors were negative. There was no support anywhere. They sneered at my eagerness to engage with experiential learning. They said, “Here, all those fancy ideas don’t work. The children are from working class backgrounds. They will only go back to be barbers and drivers. You will see.”

That day I had had enough, and was ready to give up. But then, I saw a figure hobbling on her crutches towards me. She said, “Good evening, Miss Sheila. Isn’t it a little too windy here?” We sat down to chat, away from the waves and I realized how tough life was for someone like her. The young girl, Veena, was my co-teacher. She shared that she had dreamt of becoming a trapeze artist, but she had had a fall and had lost a leg. Yet, she was sure she could do it once again, if she had an artificial foot! I admired her strength, her resilience, and the stars in her eyes. When I shared my frustration, she asked me to follow my dream. “Listen to your students, they need you,” she said. That night, I decided my job was to learn and listen, not teach and tell. Most of my class XI was street-smart. They were helping their parents and knew about the school of hard knocks. School and learning did not make any sense to them. I decided the children and I were a team then onwards. When they knew someone valued their opinions, lives, and dreams, they were ready to explore the other side. I learnt from them that teaching is more about unlearning and relearning. And I had Veena beside me, my co-teacher and lab-in-charge, to share and bounce ideas off.

This story was narrated to me by Sheila, during a teacher workshop I was conducting. I was sharing with the participants how, through my years of working with children, I had always been lucky to find a librarian or co-teacher whose ideas resonated with mine. We mentored each other through tough times. How would I describe this reciprocal or peer mentoring? Let me outline its features.

The most important features are trust and empathy. A bond needs to develop organically between the mentor and mentee. Mentoring is a conversation between two tightrope walkers and without one balancing the other, it could topple both. Another important feature is inclusion. Peer mentoring cuts across hierarchies of age, experience, class, caste, and sex. It also nurtures leadership skills in the young, and best of all, both mentor and mentee experience the joy of giving and receiving. Peer mentoring contributes to sociological theories of learning by its potential to embrace diversity.

Mentors of yesteryears

We were sitting across the library table, Vijay sir and I. He taught music to the upper primary classes. The year was 1992. I was asking him how I could use music to make learning joyful. He smiled whimsically and said, “You want learning to be fun!” And then he shared his story.

I had a passion for music. Having won many prizes in school, I set out to find a mentor, a guru. I travelled to the city and walked to the house of the greatest ustad. He took one look at me and said, “Music needs penance, and your penance is to sit outside my door for three months.” He meant to scare me away, but I was determined. For three months I sat at his doorstep listening to and practicing the divine tunes that wafted through. At last, guru mata had pity on me. I had braved the hot sun and bitter cold. She smuggled me inside. Even then, it took a year’s labour to even sit at the feet of the guru. And you want learning to be fun!

Traditionally, the concept of mentoring has painted a picture of an awe-inspiring mentor with a larger than life presence and eccentric behaviour. And the process: excruciating and demanding. The mentee is supposed to be totally devoted and rule abiding. Gone are those days, however.

Learning from the millennials

Ritu left her cushy job at the age of 56 to join an NGO. The romance of working with teachers from remote and under-resourced areas attracted her. But then, there were many bumps in the journey. This was a different world where leaders were young and savvy. Once she shed her baggage, she began to unlearn because of some amazing mentors. Working together, reading together, making presentations together, facilitating workshops together, all of these needed her to keep her eyes, ears, and heart open. Like a replanted sapling, she learnt to grow new roots, holding up in the sunlight.

Peer mentoring is an apprenticeship, sometimes between the newly minted mentor and the experienced mentee. Working together with a young person with fresh perspective and skills leads to understanding a different way of doing things, in the changing times where skills change overnight, technologies traverse distance and time. Peer mentoring contributes to learning theories of apprenticeship by nurturing change management spaces. And if your mentor is younger than you and comes with a different set of skills, it takes the form of reverse mentoring.

A need to share

Shahnaz was a young teacher, working in a rural, remote school. She was trying different ways of teaching children to read and ask critical questions that would showcase their understanding. She tried every method in the book. She modelled the questions working with Blooms taxonomy, she carefully selected picture books that should have naturally lent themselves to critical thinking, she started ‘a question a day’ bulletin board. Nothing worked! She would have given up if she had not met Shahin, who invited her to her classroom, which was in a nearby school. Shahnaz was at first disconcerted as there was nothing but noise in the class. The children were very busy and hadn’t even noticed them coming in. When her surprise settled in, Shahnaz saw that the children were sitting in well-organized groups, and one or two of them were moving between groups. On questioning them, she realized that the children were doing reciprocal teaching, using real stories from their villages, and trying to find solutions to them. When Shahnaz sat with the groups, she found them ready to share some very bold solutions to the issues they were encountering. For example, Rinku’s sister wanted to join football coaching but her parents were against it. Some children suggested that Rinku should rebel, others were more practical, and still others had win-win solutions. They had actually written a report which they presented with much questioning from other groups. Shahnaz and Shahin soon started to keep in touch, mentoring each other to break out of the isolation that teachers often feel. In rural areas, distances, terrain, weather conditions, and even internet fluctuations impose an isolation on teachers. In these conditions, peer mentoring helps build a learning community.

A learning community

The COVID pandemic ushered in a new era where the education and training world had to move online and navigate remote learning. Isolation created opportunity and experts offered free courses. This was the time when mentoring relationships between like-minded peers developed. Nanda was a young preschool head, highly educated and in tune with the latest developments. Anita was a psychologist, Anne headed the senior section in a school, and I, the oldest of them all, had retired and was at a loose end. We met regularly for several months to do action research. None of us had done action research before. We were all navigating a new learning space, all of us being teachers and students at the same time. We paired up to do action research on making educational spaces more inclusive and reflective. We mentored each other by being critical friends from the process of ideating, onboarding through reflection and action. We followed the ideation – questioning-reflection-action-reflection cycle. The end process was writing our action research papers. In this, we went through several rounds of questioning. Unsettling questions from peers – ‘What is your objective here? Is your end goal storytelling, reflection, orcritical thinking? Are you putting the cart before the horse? Would the children you are engaging with be interested in picture stories?’ – made me think and rethink. It took a lot of courage for me to moult skins of perceived expertise in my subject and teaching skill. Peer mentoring gently unleavens the so called aura of expertise and allows you to be born again!

So, how does the relationship of peer mentoring change both the mentor and the mentee? The mentee experiences light bulb moments in doing something outside their comfort zone and thus sheds the baggage of the ‘trying to prove right' syndrome. The mentor has to keep a careful balance of respect and critical reflection to make the relationship work. This acrobatic balance helps the younger mentor develop her leadership muscle and acquire new mentoring skills.

Nurturing empathy – light in the darkness

Peer or reciprocal mentoring is a learning engagement, which is a two-way-street with many twists and turns. Everybody is considerate and respectful to their elders, to experts, to leaders, but it was Swati, a young teacher, who taught me how important it is to consciously be respectful and considerate of one’s helpers. I was mentoring her on writing her action research paper on nurturing empathy among school children for their helpers. While reading her notes and having conversations with her, I had insights into the depth of her feeling for those whom most of us consider or render invisible. It was like a slow lighting of a lamp in a dark room. Things came into my range of vision as the light grew. I learnt a valuable lesson in empathy.

This is when peer mentoring manifested as reverse mentoring. It can be navigated through reading, writing, and sharing. It is often organic and seamless. The mentee experiences moments of being mentored, without the mentor realizing that she is actually changing something in an everlasting manner.

Embracing diversity

Rituparna Neog and I met in Goa during a Library Educators Course meet, held with much trepidation during the COVID pandemic. I had never seen someone as gentle and scholarly as her. She is a social activist and library educator. Her passion and scholarship, her vast work in the free library space came across strongly. It dissolved the strangeness of her being a transgender into awe. Through our conversations, I learnt a lot about her work. When I facilitated a course on critical thinking through picture books with school teachers, I felt that critical thinking was incomplete without a deep dive into spaces we have never visited. I called Rituparna to mentor the teachers and talk about her work. She revealed to them how her community struggles with legal issues, as laws never acknowledge their existence. Transgender voices are made invisible. Without social support, livelihood, education, careers, and jobs are tough going. Most of the teachers in my course on critical thinking were much older than Rituparna, and had perhaps never encountered someone like her. They were impressed and inspired. One of them, an elderly lady, translated her learning into action. She went to the transgender basti in her town, talked to them, and helped them gain employment. They were supported in gaining professional skills and soon became part of a workforce through her efforts.

Here, the reverse mentoring that happened was so powerful that it created role models for both the oppressed and the privileged in practising empathy. Moreover, it led to positive action in creating inclusive spaces. The empowerment that a teacher feels through a life-changing experience like that, transfers to her students. For, lessons in inclusion and empathy cannot be taught, they are passed on and picked up through cultural experiences.

Towards a concept of peer mentoring

Traditionally, mentoring used to be a one-way street, where learning was transferred from a well-respected mentor to a younger novice mentee. But paradigm shifts happened as overnight shift to online spaces changed the world. Peer mentoring brought in communities of practice and allowed for networks of like-minded peers to develop organically. Peer mentoring brought in multiple perspectives. Reverse mentoring is when a younger, less experienced person mentors a more experienced mentee. The mentee acquires fresh perspectives, understands multiple viewpoints, and feels a value addition. In the process, both mentor and mentee benefit from the interaction. Reverse mentoring is a value-based emotionally breaking process for both the mentor and mentee who shed their initial reluctance and baggage to acquire new skills, create knowledge, and feel empathy.

Reference

Raghavan, N. (Ed.). (2024). A Learning Community of Reflective Teachers: From Whispers to Resonance (1st ed.).

Nivedita Bedadur is happiest when she is with children and teachers, learning, unlearning, and teaching. Over years of mentoring and leading teacher capacity development, she has refined her listening and relearning muscle. Presently, she is researching for her upcoming book on the lives of the marginalized people of Konkan. She can be reached at nitavbedadur@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nivedita Bedadur
Can a mentor also be a learner? Nivedita Bedadur writes about how peer mentoring breaks hierarchies by promoting listening and unlearning, and nurtures empathy by enabling teachers to work towards a shared purpose.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: thanks Mam, for a very good article. issues discussed by are very relevant and it gives a new perspectives to the reader.

item.title: Building soft skills through art: a new approach to visual language in early education
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aditi Jain

Art is more than just a creative outlet; it is a visual language that fosters soft skills, builds emotional intelligence, and enhances cognitive abilities from a young age. As an art teacher with years of experience working with young learners, I’ve witnessed the transformative power of art. It's not just about creating pretty pictures or keeping children engaged with a fun activity; it's about developing essential soft skills that are vital for their overall growth and future success. It is a visual language – a way for children to express, communicate, and connect with the world around them.

The learning journey: more than just drawing

During a lesson on "Expressing Emotions Using Colours", young learners were encouraged to explore how different colours relate to emotions and how they can use this understanding to convey their feelings through art. The journey began with a simple song on feelings, "Scribble Spot," which allowed students to discuss their thoughts and connect emotionally. From there, they engaged in group discussions about famous artworks, relating colours to emotions and drawing on their own life experiences to make connections.

Students were then tasked with creating artworks that expressed specific emotions, focusing on the use of colours, lines, and shapes. This was not just an art activity; it was a deeply reflective process that required them to tap into their creativity, think critically about their choices, and communicate their ideas clearly.

Developing soft skills through art

This approach to art education goes beyond teaching students how to draw or paint. It helps them build critical soft skills from a young age:

  1. Emotional intelligence: By connecting colours with emotions, students learn to identify and articulate their feelings. This understanding is crucial for developing empathy and effective communication. One student, for example, chose dark blues and sharp lines to express sadness, while another used bright yellows and swirls to convey happiness. This exercise allowed them to express their emotions visually and helped them recognize and respect their peers' emotions.
  2. Creative thinking and problem-solving: Art challenges students to think outside the box and find unique solutions. In our sessions, students experimented with various art tools – crayons, colour pencils, and markers – displaying a controlled use of these mediums to bring their ideas to life. For instance, one student demonstrated exceptional control and detail in their use of colour pencils, blending hues to achieve the perfect representation of calmness in a sunset.
  3. Communication skills: Art as a visual language teaches children that communication is not limited to words. Through their drawings, students were able to express complex ideas and narratives. During presentations, they spoke about their artwork, explaining the elements they used and reflecting on the effectiveness of their expression. This process helped them build confidence and hone their public speaking skills.
  4. Perseverance and self-management: Creating art requires patience and persistence. Whether it’s mastering a new technique or revising a drawing that didn’t turn out as expected, students learn to manage their time, stay organized, and keep going despite challenges. In our class, students were encouraged to reflect on their own work using a checklist and later a descriptive rubric, assessing their progress and setting goals for improvement. This self-assessment fosters a growth mindset, teaching them that learning is a continuous journey.

Art as a visual language: a paradigm shift

Traditionally, art has been seen as extra or as a means to make learning more enjoyable. However, by viewing art as a visual language, we recognize its true potential as a powerful tool for communication and personal development. This innovative approach requires a shift in perspective – from seeing art as merely supplementary to appreciating it as a core component of education that builds essential life skills.

Imagine a classroom where art is not just for the "creative" kids, but a universal medium that all students use to explore their thoughts, convey ideas, and connect with others. This approach doesn't just prepare them for art-related careers; it prepares them for life.

Art education should not be confined to drawing and painting; it should be about encouraging young minds to explore their inner worlds and express their understanding of the world around them. When we elevate art to the status of a visual language, we empower children to communicate in diverse and profound ways, laying a strong foundation for both personal and academic success.

By integrating these practices into the curriculum from a young age, we are not just teaching students how to create art; we are teaching them how to think, feel, and communicate more effectively. In doing so, we are nurturing the next generation of thinkers, creators, and leaders who can see beyond the surface and connect with the world at a deeper level.

Art is not just about making things look beautiful; it's about making connections, expressing emotions, and understanding perspectives. Let's embrace this innovative approach and watch our students flourish as they learn to use art as their visual language.

Aditi Jain is a passionate visual arts educator with over nine years of experience, specializing in inquiry-based and student-centered learning. She fosters creativity, conceptual understanding, and artistic confidence in young learners, integrating multiple modes of expression. Committed to innovation, she explores inclusive, process-driven art education that empowers every child. She can be reached at jaditi146@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aditi Jain
Art is more than a fun break; it’s a visual language that helps children grow vital life skills. Aditi Jain writes that thoughtful art education can include group reflections, expressive sketches and more.

item.title: Reclaiming indigenous wisdom to future-proof education
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

As the world accelerates through rapid technological advancements, climate crises, and social transformations, our educational systems face a defining challenge: how to stay relevant for future generations. The answer may not lie solely in cutting-edge digital tools or performance metrics but in something far more ancient – indigenous wisdom. Deeply rooted in centuries of lived experience and grounded in a profound connection to nature, community, and culture, indigenous knowledge systems offer a holistic, sustainable, and deeply human framework for rethinking education.

Indigenous wisdom is not simply a remnant of the past – it is a living, evolving repository of cultural, ecological, and spiritual insights passed down through generations. It contrasts sharply with the fragmented and often compartmentalized knowledge dispensed in conventional classrooms. At the core of indigenous pedagogy lies the principle of interconnectedness: between people and planet, mind and body, learning and life.

One of the most significant strengths of indigenous education is its emphasis on experiential learning. For example, students may learn mathematics through traditional art forms like Warli or Gond painting, which incorporate symmetry, patterns, and geometry. Similarly, biology and ecology can come alive through hands-on activities like seed preservation, medicinal plant identification, or sustainable farming – techniques used for centuries by tribal communities in Jharkhand and Odisha.

Indigenous education is also inherently community-centered. Elders serve as "living libraries," passing down oral histories, survival skills, and value systems that form the bedrock of identity and belonging. These elders are not just teachers but custodians of wisdom who instil reverence for life and intergenerational continuity.

Equally critical is the preservation of indigenous languages, each of which embodies unique worldviews and environmental knowledge. Languages like Gondi, Bhili, or Santali are rich in ecological metaphors and geographical insights that mainstream curricula rarely capture.

Despite its richness, integrating indigenous knowledge into mainstream education is fraught with challenges. The most enduring obstacle is the colonial legacy that has long marginalized indigenous epistemologies. For decades, tribal knowledge systems were dismissed as “primitive” or “unscientific,” creating a hierarchy where Western paradigms dominate education policy and curriculum design.

Another hurdle is the curricular vacuum regarding indigenous contributions to science, medicine, and the arts. School textbooks often ignore or undervalue the sophisticated astronomical knowledge of tribal elders, the architectural ingenuity of bamboo housing, or the climate resilience inherent in traditional water harvesting techniques.

Moreover, language loss continues to be a pressing concern. The dominance of English and other global languages in educational spaces contributes to the erosion of indigenous voices, silencing centuries of wisdom encoded in dialects and idioms.

Encouragingly, several educational models in India demonstrate how indigenous knowledge can be respectfully and effectively integrated into learning ecosystems.

Eklavya Model Residential Schools (EMRS) have been pioneering the merging of tribal knowledge with formal curricula. Here, students might learn astronomy not from textbooks alone but through intergenerational conversations with village elders about moon cycles, stars, and agricultural seasons.

At the Barefoot College in Rajasthan, tribal and rural women – many of them grandmothers – are trained in solar engineering using visual learning tools and vernacular languages. These women not only electrify their communities, but also pass on their newfound expertise through oral instruction, reversing traditional flows of knowledge and power.

The Adivasi Academy in Gujarat focuses on language revival through storytelling and art. Children are taught to read and write in Bhili or Gondi using narratives that reflect their lived realities. This fosters literacy while building cultural pride.

These efforts are not fringe experiments; they are transformative case studies with implications for policy, teacher training, and community engagement.

Indigenous knowledge can enrich every subject area:

  • Science becomes tangible when students build bamboo charcoal water filters inspired by tribal methods.
  • Mathematics is visualized through the symmetrical patterns of traditional floor murals.
  • Social science is grounded in local migration stories and oral histories shared by elders.
  • Environmental studies gain urgency and depth when informed by sustainable tribal farming practices and conservation ethics.

Globally, there is a renewed interest in embedding indigenous wisdom into education systems. In Australia, for instance, Aboriginal ecological knowledge* has been woven into the environmental science curricula to teach sustainability from a localized perspective. Programs such as the Nawayee Center School in the United States serve Native American students by offering safe, culturally affirming environments where critical thinking is nurtured alongside tribal traditions.

Recent global events have further underscored the value of this wisdom. In February 2025, UNESCO released a policy brief urging countries to “decolonize curricula and embrace indigenous epistemologies to address climate change and educational equity.” The document emphasized that indigenous communities are not just passive recipients of aid but active agents of sustainability and resilience – roles they’ve played for generations.

Moreover, the indigenous Sámi community in Norway has gained attention for its snow-weather lexicon, which scientists are now using to understand micro-climatic patterns – proving that indigenous linguistic insights can have cutting-edge scientific applications.

Incorporating indigenous wisdom into education is not about romanticizing the past – it is about forging a resilient, inclusive, and sustainable future. As the climate crisis deepens and mental health challenges among the youth surge, the values embedded in indigenous teachings – community care, mindfulness, reverence for nature, and the intergenerational ethic – are more relevant than ever.

Such integration supports:

  • Holistic development: By blending academic skills with emotional, spiritual, and cultural intelligence, students become more grounded and empathetic.
  • Cultural relevance: Seeing one's heritage reflected in classroom content boosts self-esteem and cultivates a sense of belonging.
  • Innovation through tradition: Many indigenous practices – like permaculture, herbal medicine, or architecture – are inherently innovative and climate-smart.
  • Ecological literacy: Young people gain a deeper understanding of their environment and their responsibility toward it.

To truly embed indigenous wisdom, schools can begin with the following action points:

  • Map out local traditions or practices that can be tied to existing curriculum goals.
  • Invite tribal elders, folk artists, and knowledge holders into classrooms for storytelling, demonstrations, or co-teaching modules.
  • Push for curricular reforms that acknowledge tribal contributions to science, history, and the arts.
  • Develop training programs for teachers in culturally responsive pedagogy, equipping them to navigate and integrate diverse knowledge systems respectfully.

These small shifts can catalyze broader changes in how we define education and whom we include in that definition.

In a time when education systems worldwide are grappling with relevance, inclusion, and sustainability, indigenous wisdom offers more than inspiration – it offers a roadmap. By rooting pedagogy in the richness of community traditions, schools can become not just centres of learning but of healing and transformation. As Mahatma Gandhi once said, "A nation's culture resides in the hearts and in the soul of its people." To teach that culture is to teach life.

Now, more than ever, embracing indigenous wisdom isn’t optional. It’s essential.

* https://www.clc.org.au/indigenous-ecological-knowledge/

https://australiancurriculum.edu.au/TeacherBackgroundInfo?id=56676

https://australianstogether.org.au/assets/Curriculum-Resources/Y5-SciEconGeog-ACSHE083-HASS120-112-Teacher-Guide.pdf

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A TEDx speaker and certified virtual presenter, she assists multinational companies, educational institutions, and the armed forces to bridge generational gaps. An Oxford University Press Resource Person, she is pursuing a Senior Management Executive Program at IIM Trichy. You can connect with her https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
Indigenous wisdom is much more than folk tales and rituals. Neerja Singh writes how it is a living, evolving guide to reimagining learning for a resilient future.

item.title: Teaching hope: why it matters now more than ever
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nirupama Kaushik

In March 2025, Netflix premiered a show titled Adolescence. Set in the UK, the narrative centers around a murder committed by a 13-year-old boy succumbing to peer pressure. As the investigation unfolds, viewers are drawn into the shadowy realms of the virtual world and the complexities of social media, confronting the challenges that define today's childhood – marked by elevated anxiety levels, peer pressures, and diminished self-esteem. While critics of the series noted the discomforting nature of its premise, educators may have found it to be a portrayal reflective of their own experiences. Teachers, while fortunate to witness many moments of joy, are often the only adults in the workforce who encounter the indifference, rebellion, withdrawal, anger, and anguish of a generation increasingly lost behind screens, struggling with societal expectations and mental health challenges. Despite these hurdles, we continue to show up each day. But how do we persevere, and more importantly, what motivates us to do so?

To be an educator is to be hopeful. Lakshmi Karunakaran’s article, the first in this series, thoroughly unpacks this notion with experiences and details. But what is said to be an intrinsic belief with a vision for a positive future is disappearing from the classroom. Hope is no longer instinctive. It is a practice that needs to be modelled. A set of tools that needs to be acquired as students grow. A relationship with oneself and the world that needs to be nurtured deliberately. And it begins with teachers.

Why hope?

UNICEF’s The State of The World’s Children Report 2024 lays emphasis on “The Future Of Childhood In A Changing World”. The report explores three megatrends that will “profoundly affect children’s lives between now and 2050” and these are demographic shifts, climate and environmental crises, and frontier technologies. As per a review1 published by NIMHANS in 2024, approximately 23.33% of school children in India were reported to have mental health issues. Depression was identified as the most prevalent issue among students along with other contributors like social, behavioural, and emotional problems, post-pandemic learning issues, anxiety, psychological distress, internet addiction, stress, and attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder. While this psychological fatigue looms large, it also points at an emotionally vulnerable generation that perhaps cannot imagine an alternative future. It is necessary for teachers to step in to bridge these gaps and rekindle hope – not as blind optimism but to empower children with agency to turn the situation around. Because if we don’t, who will?

How?

At the outset, it is important to understand that one size does not fit all. Every child possesses different abilities, interests, and experiences, and it is the responsibility of the teacher to acknowledge this and tap into each student’s unique potential. While Dr. C. R. Snyder’s Hope Theory deals with three parts – goals, pathways, and agency – there are other frameworks that one can also look at:

  • Dr. Martin Seligman’s PERMA™ Theory that explores the question of what is human flourishing and what enables it. The building blocks to achieve a better life are Positive Emotion, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, and Accomplishment (PERMA™) and Positive Psychology focuses on providing strategies to individuals to flourish, aligning with their independent goals.
  • Dr. Jeff Duncan-Andrade’s concept of hope moves away from the follies of “false hope” and replaces it with “critical hope” which is grounded in action and genuine engagement in addressing social injustices. Teachers are advocates in students’ journeys towards collective good.
  • Narrative psychology concerns itself with the role of stories and storytelling in adding value to an individual’s experience. According to Dan P. McAdams, “A narrative identity is a person’s internalized and evolving story of how he or she has become the person he or she is becoming”(McAdams and McLean 2013). It is the story we tell ourselves about who we are, based on our past experiences, present life, and hopes for the future. We bridge the gap between what has happened and what is possible in the future.

What stands out across all these frameworks is the shared understanding that hope is a transferable skill. It can be embedded in daily classroom teaching and taught to students to make them more resilient.

Using these frameworks, the following section uses multiple lenses to shape our perspectives on hope.

Hope as connection and belonging

Human beings are hardwired cognitively, emotionally, physically, and socially to belong to something larger than themselves. A break in that connection leads to a sense of isolation and hopelessness. Sheetal Vora, who has worked in the field of early childhood education for over 14 years, says, “When children step out of their homes and into the world, their first step is often school. At home, they feel safe, comfortable, and secure. They are the center of attention and surrounded by familiar love. That deep sense of belonging becomes their foundation. But when they enter a classroom for the first time, their comfort is gently shaken. No familiar faces to begin with – just strangers in a new environment. In that moment, the responsibility of creating a new sense of safety, comfort, and connection falls on their first teacher. It is this hope – that drives the teacher’s every action.

Circle time becomes one of the most powerful tools to nurture hope. It is where connections are formed, and where every child has a chance to be heard and seen. A simple question like, “What’s your favourite toy?” invites each child to share, making their presence visible. When the teacher responds with, “I love that toy too!” or asks with curiosity “what is that?”, it creates a moment of joyful recognition. That simple acknowledgment opens the door to emotional safety and comfort and with that seeds of trust are sown. The hopeful thought that was so far abstract turned into a positive tangible experience that will not only remain etched for a long time but also nurture self-confidence.”

One can only imagine the transformative power of these repetitive experiences.

Hope through recognition and relevance

When I first started out as a teacher 15 years ago, I grappled with a question – How could a standard curriculum and textbook truly reflect the lives of my class of 40 when each child had a rich inner life separate and different from the other? Where was the space for their individuality to shine? I addressed this simply by listening deeply to their stories and learning about their culture, languages and what caught their attention and interests. When I moved on to becoming a library educator, I would return to this thought often and work on building a space and collection of books where children felt seen, heard, and valued. JoAnne Saldanha reiterates the importance and power of stories in building hope in children. Recognition and relevance lead to visibility. Visibility nurtures hope.

Hope as emotional safety

Mansi Nanavati, a school counsellor based in Mumbai, shares how emotional safety is paramount in a school. “Children spend a large portion of their day at school with their friends and teachers. That means everyone in the school plays a part in helping them feel hopeful and bounce back from tough times. We can build strong, hopeful places for students by building student agency– when students are truly involved in setting class goals, they feel like it's their learning, and they care more; helping them collaborate and shine together – when they get chances to work together, they learn from each other's strengths and pull together to reach a goal as a group. This builds connections and support; and lastly, teaching students to see the humorous side to their mistakes. It helps them not to be so hard on themselves and to actually enjoy learning, bumps and all.”

Creating safe and respectful spaces gives hope a chance to emerge.

Hope as agency

It is unacceptable to present a narrative about tomorrow without showing children that they can be the change they wish to see in the world. With the right tools, children can alter their future. In close to 70 villages near Bhopal, under the aegis of Eklavya Foundation’s Library and Reading Initiative (LARI), children are trained to run “Chakmak Clubs” – informal community libraries where they cultivate a love for reading. The striking feature of this model is the importance given to the agency of the children who have complete freedom to explore and experiment. Right from planning to execution, the children take charge of the club while the adult library educator is an observer. A vibrant collection of books is made available to children of different ages and interests and every evening, the group participates in songs, stories, and other activities centered around books. Beyond school, the club becomes a space to connect with each other and become more aware of the world around them. This leads to discussions on community issues, gender, empathy, and many more concepts. The children are confident, open to learning and display remarkable conflict resolution skills. Agency nurtures hope. When children are invited to co-create knowledge, solve problems, and act on their environments, they learn that their voices and choices matter.

Hope in the absence of hope

What can a teacher do when children are vulnerable and feel the weight of the world on their shoulders – when they are too overburdened to feel hopeful? Nirmayee Sanghvi from Down To Earth who works with children from under-resourced communities in Mumbai shares a story about one of her students. “He was a bright student, but in Grade 11, an epileptic fit changed everything. A nerve in his brain burst. Though the operation was successful and he did not lose sensation, he lost something deeper – hope. Fear took over. He was too scared to study, convinced he would never pass Grade 12. As his teachers, we knew sympathy was not going to get him anywhere. We chose belief and firmness. We encouraged him, reminded him of who he was, and helped him find the strength he had forgotten.

He cleared his board exams with 50%. It was not about the marks. It was a triumph of belief over fear.”

When students lose hope, a teacher has to hold it for them until they are ready to hold it themselves.

From abstract to concrete – what can schools do?

Cultivating hope requires more than motivation. It requires three active commitments – Helping students imagine a different future, equipping them with the tools to pursue it, and standing by them when the process gets hard. It is also essential to remember that students do not enter the classroom as blank slates. They carry with them a diversity of lived experiences shaped by caste, class, gender, religion, language, disability, and neurodiversity. Hope cannot be raised in absence of these realities. For many children, especially those from historically marginalized or underrepresented communities, simply imagining a better future requires dismantling internalized barriers and external biases.

Design a hope-centric curriculum

A hope-centric curriculum that encourages collaboration over competition, the development of abilities over marks and grades, and leading learning with questions that push students to think critically gives them the resources to aspire and take action. Keeping their contexts in mind, use materials – print and non-print – that focus on resilience, community building, and overcoming difficulties. Make space for imagination and not just instruction.

Use diverse modes of expression

Provide students with the vocabulary and tools to build a mindset that is set in hope. Many children may not be able to articulate how they think or feel in words. This causes them to clam up and not participate. But as educators, if we give them opportunities to share through art, music, and movement, they will be able to externalize and reframe their doubts, fears, and anxieties. Once the emotion is named, it can be moved.

Reframe praise

According to Carol Dweck, the author of Mindset: The New Psychology of Success, “The wrong kind of praise creates self-defeating behavior. The right kind motivates students to learn.” Instead of praising intelligence, if we look at praising effort, we are building students’ abilities to stick with a process rather than a product of achievement. When students learn that intelligence can be built through small, consistent efforts, they are ready to walk down that path. So next time, instead of praising only the student with the right answer, praise the child who overcame self-doubt and tried something new or used a different strategy to attempt a math problem.

Prioritize teacher wellbeing

Teachers, like all other human beings, cannot pour from an empty cup. Low salaries, lack of work-life balance, and recognition along with burnout, has led to the phenomenon called “Quiet Quitting”, especially post pandemic. It reflects teachers’ dissatisfaction and disengagement from the work. It is imperative that schools take the mental and physical health of teachers seriously and create opportunities for peer connections, calm reflection, and invest in their professional and emotional wellbeing. Systemic changes must be made for teachers to feel a sense of ownership towards the vision of the school with room for dialogue between all stakeholders instead of a top-down approach. Hopeful teachers nurture hopeful students.

In Adolescence, we are shown a world where a child slips through the cracks; lost in a maze of digital validation, peer pressure, and isolation. It is a haunting reminder of what happens when young people are left to navigate complexity without informed choices. As educators, we cannot turn away. We are anchors, mirrors, bridges, and builders of possibility. In a time where despair is often more visible than dreams, holding space for hope is not extra. It is essential – essential to show students that a better future can be imagined, built, and believed in. And that they do not have to do it alone. We are right there with them – one conversation, one story, one child at a time.

References

  1. Balamurugan G, Sevak S, Gurung K, et al. (May 25, 2024) Mental Health Issues Among School Children and Adolescents in India: A Systematic Review. Cureus 16(5): e61035. DOI 10.7759/cureus.61035.
  2. McAdams, D. P. (2019). “First we invented stories, then they changed us”: The Evolution of Narrative Identity. Evolutionary Studies in Imaginative Culture, 3(1), 1-18. https://doi.org/10.26613/esic.3.1.110
  3. https://www.eklavya.in/about-us-eklavya/what-we-do-eklavya-new/702-library-and-reading-initiative-lari
  4. Dweck, C. S. (2008). The perils and promises of praise. Best of Educational Leadership 2007-2008, Volume 65, 34-39. https://teaching.temple.edu/sites/teaching/files/resource/pdf/Dweck-Perils%20%26%20Promises%20of%20Praise.pdf
  5. Konal Memiş, T., Tabancalı, E., & Yıldız Technical University. (2024). Teachers’ silent scream: Quiet quitting. In Research in Educational Administration & Leadership (Vols. 372-412, p. 373). https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1444201.pdf

Nirupama Kaushik is a Library Educator with over 11 years of experience in education, leadership, and library development. She mentors librarians at The Somaiya School and leads initiatives to promote reading and writing beyond the classroom. She is also closely associated with the children’s section at Kitab Khana and co-anchors the Librarians of India community, committed to strengthening library practices across the country. She can be reached at nirupama.kaushik@somaiya.edu.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nirupama Kaushik
What does it mean to teach hope, not just content? In this last article in our Hope Series, Nirupama Kaushik writes why hope might be the most vital lesson of all in today’s classrooms.

item.title: Empowering the future: vocational skills in NEP 2020
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Mrunal Raut

The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 has emerged as a landmark reform in India's education system, aiming to transform learning from the traditional, theory-heavy model into a holistic andskill-oriented experience. Among its many progressive provisions, the policy's strong emphasis on vocational education stands out as a vital step towards building a skilled and self-reliant nation.

Recognizing the long-standing disconnect between academic knowledge and employability, NEP 2020 envisions an education system that equips students not only with theoretical knowledge but also with practical, real-world skills. It proposes the integration of vocational training from as early as grade 6, with an aim to expose students to various skill-based activities and real-life work environments through internships and hands-on experiences.

A new approach to learning

Unlike the conventional approach where vocational training was offered only at the highersecondary or post-secondary level, NEP 2020 introduces skill-based learning in middle school itself. This early exposure helps students identify their interests and aptitudes at a formative age. The policy advocates for at least one vocational course during grades 6 to 8, supplemented by a 10-day internship with local vocational experts such as carpenters, gardeners, potters, artisans, and others.

In addition, NEP 2020 aims to remove the stigma traditionally associated with vocational education. By giving it equal importance as academic subjects and integrating it into the formal curriculum, the policy promotes dignity of labour and encourages students to pursue skill-based careers without

hesitation.

A diverse skill ecosystem

The vocational courses under NEP 2020 are designed to be both locally relevant and nationally significant. Schools are encouraged to collaborate with local craftsmen, NGOs, industry partners, and community members to offer skills such as:

  • Electric work and carpentry
  • Coding and computer literacy
  • Gardening and organic farming
  • Tailoring and embroidery
  • Pottery and handicrafts
  • Entrepreneurship and business basics

These skills are not just vocational in the traditional sense but also include emerging 21stcentury competencies, preparing students for jobs that are rapidly evolving in today's digital and global economy.

Building a skilled nation

The integration of vocational skills within the mainstream education system is expected to yield far-reaching benefits:

  • Enhanced employability: Students will leave school with tangible skills that increase their chances of getting jobs or starting their own ventures.
  • Reduced dropout rates: Engaging, practical education is more likely to retain students who may

otherwise lose interest in rote learning.

  • Entrepreneurial spirit: With the right exposure, students are more likely to explore self-employment and innovation-driven careers.
  • Self-reliant India: The vision of "Atmanirbhar Bharat" gets a strong boost through the creation of a skilled workforce aligned with national and global demands.

The road ahead

For the successful implementation of vocational education, capacity building at the institutional level is crucial. This includes teacher training, development of flexible and dynamic curricula, infrastructural support, and a collaborative effort between government bodies, industries, and educational institutions.

The vocational education framework prescribed in NEP 2020 is not merely an educational reform,it is a cultural shift. It aspires to create a generation that respects all forms of work, values practical knowledge, and is prepared for the real challenges of life and work.

By reimagining the role of education in shaping a well-rounded individual, NEP 2020 brings us astep closer to a future-ready India, where every child is empowered not just to learn, but also to create, innovate, and contribute meaningfully to society.

Mrunal Raut is Academic in-charge, Pioneer Public School Solapur, Maharashtra.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mrunal Raut
STEAM subjects are not the only subjects one needs to learn if they are to succeed in life. Recognizing this, NEP 2020 has highlighted the importance of vocational skill training to integrate theory with practice and transform children into complete individuals.

item.title: The need to understand the essence of mathematics
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
R. L. Priyadarshini

As a biomedical engineer turned mathematics teacher, I have witnessed the profound role that mathematics plays in every breakthrough, from life-saving equipment to artificial intelligence. A study by the National Academy of Sciences states that mathematics plays a critical role in almost every aspect of modern science and engineering (NAS, 2020,The Role of Mathematics in Modern Science and Engineering).

Despite its obvious importance, mathematicsseems to fill students with fear, and is rarely appreciated for its artistic and creativity nature.

As mathematics teachers and educators, we must acknowledge that our classrooms can seem boring to students because we have not presented mathematics in the way it was perceived by our ancestors. They used mathematics to solve real-life problems without even realizing they were using the subject we now call "mathematics." Unfortunately, in today's classrooms, this real-world connection is not being highlighted, as students are often forced to memorize formulas and theorems instead of exploring the subject’s deeper purpose.

Even as a student of biomedical engineering, I have used mathematical concepts such as integral and differential calculus, vector algebra, and mathematical modelling to model biological systems and design medical devices. But it was when I started teaching mathematics as a part-time teacher that I first realized the basic application of mathematics and how this is not being highlighted in Indian schools.

Visiting countries like Singapore and the United States gave me another perspective towards mathematics. Students in these countries perceive mathematics by connecting it with practical applications. They use calculators to calculate, as it simplifies their work. When I studied at NTU, Singapore, where I did my MS in biomedical engineering, the question papers of mathematics based subjects like biophysics or biomechanics had formulas printed as well for use by the exam-writing students. This is  that students don’t have to memorize and remember formulas. It is not the students memory power that the institute wanted to test but our ability to apply our learning to real-life challenges.

The most innovative countries, like Switzerland, Sweden, and the USA, owe much of their advancements to STEM fields, particularly mathematics (Global Innovation Index, 2020). This also makes the careers in STEM fields grow at a more rapid pace. So, students and parents must ensure that they learn mathematics by understanding that it will empower them to survive and lead in a data-driven futuristic world.

As a former biomedical engineer turnedmathematics teacher, I firmly believe that the future will be shaped by those who understand the essence of mathematics and apply it to solve global problems. I am looking forward to that exciting future and your constructive suggestions to make mathematics a piece of cake.

R. L. Priyadarshini is currently a faculty member at Delhi Public School, Coimbatore, and a passionate theatre artist. She performs in progressive plays with groups like Chennai KalaiKuzhu and was formerly a freelance faculty member at T.I.M.E Institute, Chennai. She can be reached at priyadarshini.rl@dpscoimbatore.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
R L Priyadarshini
One of the most common phobias that you hear of is the fear of mathematics. Unfortunately, a lot of life is based on mathematics and therefore our understanding of the subject is essential. The one way to do this is to help students see the connection between the subject and real life.

item.title: Rhymes, rhymes one more time
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Amita Saundattikar

Rhymes are very useful for students. They help them perceive sounds, pronounce words and learn how to speak. They can also support the development of a number of skills:

*Rhymes teach children the sounds of language and the construction of words. Children can increase their vocabulary and reading proficiency by doing this.

*Rhymes can aid in children’s cognitive development by teaching them how to solve problems.

*Rhymes teach kids about emotions and how to express them, which will aid in their social-emotional development.

*Rhymes can also foster a strong link between children, teachers, or other adults while also assisting children to unwind and de-stress.

Apart from the popular English rhymes, rhymes in vernacular languages are also adored by kids. They are frequently employed to assist kids in learning about numbers,colours, and other ideas. They can aid kids in improving their memory and language while also being a lot of fun to sing.In short, rhymes increase interest in music and the ability to sing songs and simply help kids in their early literacy and reading skills. All teachers should conduct rhymes session every day to grab the attention of the children and make classesmore interesting and enjoyable.

AmitaSaundattikar is a pre-primary facilitator, Pawar Public School, Nanded. She can be reached at amitasaundattikar@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Amita Saundattikar
Why is it that anywhere in the world, little children are taught rhymes? What is it about rhymes that gives them their universal appeal? Let's find out.

item.title: Exploring the essence of learning
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nidhi Solanki

My method of teaching science to children evolved over a period of 10 years, transitioning from hands-on learning to inquiry-based learning. As a teacher, I frequently tested my methods with children, finding that some worked wonderfully while others did not. Sometimes working in a group benefits a child, and sometimes working alone is better. Thus, as an educator, it is essential to try different approaches to find the right balance. Trusting children, giving them ownership, or at least involving them as much as possible in the learning process is very important. While methodology is crucial, I believe the ambiance is equally important, such as displays, resources, or necessary tools. This is why I prefer to conduct my classes in the science lab rather than in a classroom.

My approach to teaching has mostly shifted from hands-on learning to inquiry-based learning. With senior classes such as 11th and 12th grades, I also use conventional methods like lectures. However, even while using the lecture method, I incorporate different resources such as simulations and experiments, and involve students as much as possible through discussions. I won't go into detail here, as the purpose of this article is to focus on the approaches I primarily use with students from primary to 10th grade.

Hands-on learning

At Anand Niketan, I mostly worked with kids on topics like light, electricity, force, and pressure. I would demonstrate an experiment or show a demo and ask the children to think about it. For example, I demonstrated an experiment of “walking water”. I took two beakers, one empty and the other filled with water. I took one piece of cloth and put one end of the cloth in the empty beaker and the other end in the beaker filled with water. I left the setup overnight. The next day, we saw what happened. There was a difference in the water levels. I asked my students why that had happened and then together we tried to understand why. The goal was not to reach the end of the reasoning, which was to find the answer to all the questions, but to engage with the questions and find the ways to find answers.

Sometimes, children would make statements that others wouldn’t understand. My approach was to simplify everything to help them truly understand what happens and why. Such discussions might take hours or even days, and to keep curiosity alive, one must continuously provide fuel, which I did by asking probing questions.

For instance, when discussing the pen in the beaker, if they said the pen bends when it enters the water, I would hold it straight and parallel to the walls of the beaker, and they would see that the pen is not bending now. I would ask questions like ‘what conditions are required to see this bend?’ or use a laser to show the same experiment. During discussions, what one child says often provides clues to others, helping us move forward. The idea is not just to reach the known solution but to observe and appreciate the questions or statements that arise, even if they are not directly related to the main question.

Here, I did not consider writing work important and did not force children to write. However, I suggested drawing diagrams and writing points to remember the discussion. Sometimes, model or project making was purely for enjoyment, like making balloon rockets or a parachute. Balloon rockets were made by attaching a balloon to a horizontal string. When blown up and released, the balloon would speedily move in one direction. Since the children didn’t ask “why”, I too never forced them to learn the reasons.

The thought behind all this was for children to have hands-on experience, as learning is more effective when the hand, heart, and head are involved. Therefore, I encouraged the children to make circuits without giving them instructions, simply providing them with a bulb, wires, a bulb holder, and a battery or cells. As they connected the components in various ways, they figured out what worked and what didn’t. This process was fascinating for both them and me. It led to a series of questions, such as why the cell must be connected a certain way, why a bulb doesn’t work when touched anywhere on its body, or why a circuit doesn’t work if rubber is used in between. These questions help us delve deeper and learn more. The role of a teacher or facilitator becomes very important here, guiding these questions in a way that leads to better understanding. This comes with experience and a lot of preparation before and after each class.

Some of the important skills involved with this type of method are observation, critical thinking, and hands-on practical skills.

Inquiry-based learning

For years, I experimented with different methods, sometimes using hands-on approaches and other times using lecture modes with videos, readings, and discussions. In each method I tried, there was an element of inquiry-based learning. Evoking curiosity and sustaining interest were common factors among all these methods. However, while working at Prakriti School in Noida, I fully implemented inquiry-based learning. Here, the ownership of learning was entirely with the students.

To understand this better, here are a few examples:

Example 1: Density

Density can often be misconstrued with weight. Inquiries such as why a ship stays afloat despite its heaviness can stimulate critical thinking. In my methodology, I present queries and encourage children to propose explanations, followed by collaborative discussions on each explanation. This method involves deduction, moving from numerous possibilities to fewer alternatives.

I devised two experiments and distributed written procedures with blank observation tables to students. Subsequent to each experiment, we deliberated on the observations and the underlying reasons. This approach, which accommodates multiple correct answers, fosters holistic thinking in children. It is imperative to acknowledge all viewpoints without imparting any sense of bias. Occasionally, I deliberately highlight certain points to instil confidence. Errors and erroneous assumptions are equally valuable in the realm of science and education. We document all viewpoints shared during discussions before transitioning to the next experiment. One experiment entailed determining the density of various items like vegetables, fruits, and stones. We noted that certain items did not submerge, prompting a discourse on buoyancy and its correlation with density. It became apparent that items with cavities or high water content tended to float. Conversely, some denser objects sank. This exploration elucidated the distinctions between mass and density. While students calculated density by applying the Archimedes principle to measure volume, a query arose regarding whether hollow containers displace the same volume when forcibly submerged. A student conducted the experiment and was elated to unveil the outcomes independently. This impromptu experiment was particularly thrilling for me as well. The underlying principle is straightforward: when a child poses a question, they contemplate the answer and proceed to test it. Regardless of whether the anticipated outcome is achieved, firsthand learning is inevitable in all scenarios.

Example 2: Electricity

Here is another example of how I tried inquiry-based learning with a different topic that was more practical. While we were covering other topics, children of class eight would often ask when we would be doing electricity, so the next topic we started was electricity, at their request. This is another thing I did: I let the children choose the topic they wanted to do next. Sometimes chapters need to be done in sequence to make more sense, but otherwise, we would do them randomly.

Preparation done before the topic: I went to different shops to buy the items I needed for the experiments. Now, one can assume that this topic can only be understood by doing, but since I was using inquiry-based learning, the idea was also to let the children think more deeply about the questions to make sense of their learning. I made groups of 4-5 children. We had about five groups. Each group was given a tray with all the materials – bulbs, wires, cells with holders, switches, etc. I had done some pre-work, such as screwing plastic wires into holders.

The idea was to ask questions and let the children find answers by working in groups. They would perform the experiments and then discuss. It was interesting to see the discussions and debates that happened in the groups. Children would then share their findings with the larger group, which would involve each member's thoughts.

For example, I asked the children how they thought the bulb in our lab was working. What things are required for it to work?

The children said there are wires running underneath the walls that connect the bulb to the switch. Another child said the switch provides the energy. A third child said it was coming from somewhere else. One child said it is coming from far away.

I then asked the children to use the materials given to light the bulb. I had told them nothing about how to connect it. Initially, there was no bulb holder or cell holder. They only had bulbs, wires, and cells. It was interesting to see how they kept trying different ways, helping each other, and shouting instructions. Obviously, there was a lot of chaos, but it was necessary chaos without which this learning couldn’t happen. I also noticed that some children were left out, and even after trying, they wouldn’t engage much with others. They were comfortable only with their friends, so I put them with their friends. Then I found them fully engaged and trying different things. Some groups found the way to light the bulb, and those who didn’t would look at other circuits and try the same, while some would keep trying their own ways. This obviously needs time, and one has to be patient. Towards the end, each group was asked to come and draw their circuits. Here, we learned to use the symbols after a discussion, as we figured out that not everyone was comfortable drawing the components.

Many of them shared their findings, and while they were sharing, I also asked questions like: Could we connect the bulb at all points on it? They said no and explained that the bulb will only light if we connect it at two specific points. Similarly, we talked about cells. This way, we discussed terminals. Then I told them to add a switch to their circuit after discussing the role of the switch.

We then talked about the ways we could connect bulbs and discussed series and parallel circuits. Here, I first asked them to think, share, and discuss in the group about what would happen if two bulbs are connected in series or parallel. It was interesting to first know their answers and then see what actually happened, and then think about why it happened. For example, the brightness of the bulbs reduces if we have two bulbs instead of one. Also, why do bulbs glow brighter in parallel circuits than in series circuits? We then tried to model electrons to understand the concept of energy in a circuit. There were many questions the children had that were obviously not answered by this model, and this was when we also discussed the idea of different models or theories in science. We also talked about measuring current and voltage using meters. Children were excited to measure and to finally have a number to associate the term with.

At last we tried answering a few questions where children would first think and then answer, after which we would verify using the PHeT simulation and discuss the answers and why they were different, if at all. It took us a long time to finish this chapter (nearly two months), but the experience of learning will never be forgotten.

While electricity was being explored in class, I gave the children a project where I asked the parents to buy a few kits and then gave some puzzles for the children to solve using the kit. Many children developed an interest because they were doing it at home with their parents or siblings and told me that their concepts were much clearer. This was also a way to involve the parents in the children's learning. Obviously, after a certain age, one might not think of involving parents in learning in this way, as children like to do their tasks all by themselves.

The important skills involved with inquiry based learning were observation, critical thinking, taking ownership of their learning, collaboration, and listening skills.

Hands-on learning vs inquiry based learning: My journey of going from hands-on learning to inquiry based learning was natural and I developed it while working with children and not by reading about it somewhere and applying it. Hands-on learning is very much part of inquiry based learning. However, it is much more than that. It is about making the learning their own by engaging with the problem statement or the question and looking for the answers.

Other elements of learning: Children learn all the time whether or not someone else is in the picture. Hence, it is important to talk about those spaces to emphasize this point. Such spaces which are not formally designed by anyone and yet they become important in the learning of the child. Some of these spaces are where I was present, while some where I wasn’t.

  • Learning in the physics lab: I believe a physics lab or a science corner is an important space for learning, so I try to build it at the beginning. Doing science in a lab has a unique learning experience: first, it provides the right ambiance, and second, it has everything you may need while doing science. Children would often come to the lab and explore the items kept there. Sometimes, while playing, they would observe something interesting. For example, while playing with a slinky, one child observed that if we drop the slinky vertically, the lower part stays unmoved until the upper part touches it, and then they move together. He shared this with the other children. We recorded it in slow motion and watched it together. This was his little discovery, and he then explained the theory behind it. Other children argued with his points, and he took some time to think. A few days later, he came up with another explanation and shared it with the others. Here, it is not important whether he got the right answer – after all, what is a right answer? The important thing is to understand the process and keep searching until you get a satisfactory explanation, which then can be considered the right answer.
  • Exploration outside the class: Sometimes, children would come with observations or questions related to something being done in the science class. For example, while discussing waves (progressive waves like sound waves and water waves) in class, a child noticed the waves formed with a rope of the curtains in the chemistry lab. He noticed that these waves started at the end of the rope he held but disappeared at the other end, unlike water waves. He shared this with me, and while discussing, he found it was a different kind of wave, which we call ‘standing waves’. He jumped with excitement, knowing he had discovered something new on his own. Science is not confined to the classroom; it is everywhere. For example, for a topic we were covering, we decided to do a treasure hunt prepared by the children of grade 10. There were two teams and four moderators. The moderators prepared the questions and planned the hunt. Each team would get a clue to move to the next question if they answered the previous question correctly. The benefit of such an activity is that children get to learn a lot from each other. While playing the treasure hunt, since each child was involved in the activity very enthusiastically, they were all very attentive. Or the other time, to understand the idea of average speed with grade 9, we used the idea of race where children ran and we found their average speeds. The whole chapter of motion was done on the playground.
  • Projects: It is a well-established fact that learning happens when you are involved and actively doing something to learn. This is why simply listening to lectures given by an expert will not help you learn unless you make an effort. Hence, I strongly believe that the role of the facilitators should be small compared to that of the learners. One time, I gave grade 9 students different questions on energy to explore. Knowing their interests, I asked one child to look at the historical aspects of energy use, another to find out about new resources, such as hydrogen fuel, and another child to perform an experiment and share his findings. They had the freedom to use any resource, conduct any experiment, and talk to any expert. It was fascinating to see that the findings the children shared had a broad perspective. The experiment of transferring energy (compounded pendulum) they demonstrated was done using easily accessible materials like rope, plastic bottles, and a table at home. Such opportunities give children the confidence not just in learning but in owning it by sharing it with others.
  • Connection of physics with the challenges humanity is facing: Humans are facing problems that need thinkers who think out-of-the-box and hence talking to children about these problems is essential. I tried to integrate them wherever possible. For example, I conducted a debate on nuclear energy with class 10 students. Students knew about the debate weeks in advance, giving them time to prepare their points for the side they were advocating. Some were in favour of using nuclear energy as it is the future and we are nearly out of fossil fuels, while others were against it, arguing that it is harmful and produces waste that cannot be managed. During the debate, a few children who hadn't read anything about it or weren't prepared, initially listened to the other students and ended up sharing their points as well. Often, students who haven't opted to join in the physics class, while listening, share their points too. This kind of discussion not only enriches one's knowledge but provokes deeper thinking.

All these examples show that the children are relating science to their lives and seeing it around them. For me, this is what true learning is.

Nidhi Solanki is an educator and researcher with extensive experience in alternative education. She has worked with NGOs like Eklavya and Azim Premji Foundation (APF), focusing on improving science education in government and alternative schools. She has taught at Anand Niketan Democratic School, KFI Rajghat Besant School, and the inclusive Prakriti School in Noida, where she implemented Inquiry-Based and Project-Based Learning approaches to foster curiosity and critical thinking. She has actively engaged in designing hands-on learning experiences, facilitating teacher development, and addressing challenges in science pedagogy. Passionate about social justice and public policy, she continues to engage in interdisciplinary research. She is also an amateur birdwatcher and a published writer. She can be reached at solankinidhi34@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nidhi Solanki
From walking water to self-built circuits, Nidhi Solanki’s approach shows how questions – not answers – drive scientific learning.

item.title: Empowering students through the performing arts: a holistic approach
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Abhishek Panchal and Nishna Mehta

Founded in 2012, The Gateway School of Mumbai (GSoM) is a not-for-profit institution dedicated to empowering children with learning challenges. Collaborating with students, families, and our community of educators, we strive to enable students to lead fulfilling, productive lives and become active contributors to society. GSoM functions as a learning lab, excelling in educational practice and innovation. We prioritize the arts in our core curriculum to foster holistic development, artistic literacy, and integration with language, humanities, and the sciences through Multi-Disciplinary Learning Units (MLUs). Our goal is to ensure meaningful engagement with the arts, making student learning visible through various formal and informal opportunities to showcase their creative potential.

Programme overview

The programmes at GSoM are developmentally appropriate and tailored to each child's learning needs, using a strength-based approach. Students are grouped by age and ability. In the lower school (ages 7-11), the focus is on foundational skills like language, literacy, math, social and emotional development, and sensory-motor skills. Middle school (ages 11-14) continues to build these skills while introducing adaptive strategies for those needing additional support. In high school (ages 14+), the emphasis shifts to preparing students for life beyond GSoM, collaborating with parents to ensure students meet the prerequisites for future success.

The performing arts programme

Overview

Our performing arts programme offers a progressive curriculum tailored to students' developmental needs and learning stages.
