Its success is due to the collaboration between the Speech Therapy, Social-Emotional, and Sensory-Motor departments of the school, and its focus on body awareness, motor skills, and multi-sensory experiences. Critical thinking and real-world problem-solving skills are taught through various performing arts, including improvisation, puppetry, pantomime, role-play, sensory theatre, reader’s theatre, and street play. The program also provides a safe space for students to explore and express their emotions and creativity. The following sections will detail how the program catered to lower, middle, and high school students during the 2023-24 academic year.

Approach

In the 2023-24 academic year, teachers engaged students in creative learning experiences through performance-making. While broader themes were decided by the teaching team, the content nuances were driven by students' explorations and opinions. This approach fostered student agency in the classroom, rehearsals, and performances. Performing Arts Education at GSoM combines process-oriented and production-focused theatre-making, benefiting children with disabilities in areas like speech clarity, motor skills, impulse control, adaptability, and emotional regulation.

Andy Kempe*, in his chapter “Drama and the Education of Young People with Special Needs” from the book Key Concepts in Theatre/Drama Education, states, “The use of drama in the curriculum for children with special needs is sometimes automatically equated with drama therapy. Such an assumption may arise from the mistaken belief that if a child has some kind of learning disability, the only type of drama of any value for them must be designed to help them with their particular individual need.” Breaking this assumption, we at GSoM recognize that the performing arts are a social art form. They not only develop inter-personal skills like public speaking, communication, and confidence, but also intra-personal skills like building a worldview, forming opinions, coping with change, and problem-solving. To achieve this, students participated in group discussions, field trips, and explorative play, driving the creative process collectively.

Lower school: a focus on foundational skills such as spatial awareness and fine motor skills

Lower school students engaged in shadow play using their body and hands. Students with special learning needs often struggle with vision processing and motor difficulties, impacting their spatial awareness – a key skill for daily activities. Shadow play requires awareness of one’s position relative to other players, the light source, and a shadow screen. In one of the performances, students successfully transitioned between performing in front of and behind the screen in the dark with regular practice. This exercise introduces foundational concepts of light and shadow, helping students connect abstract shadows with the concrete objects creating them. Another exercise involved acting as the shadow of a player in front of the screen, demonstrating an understanding of two players portraying one character – one as the character and the other as its shadow. Shadow play also accommodates students who prefer staying behind the screen and encourages non-verbal communication. Additionally, the performances of lower school students illustrate kindness, a fundamental quality for inclusion.

Middle school: A focus on experiential learning

Performance-making integrates research and experiential learning, enriching students’ knowledge about various subjects, including their immediate surroundings. Middle school students went on field trips to better understand the context of their plays. Reflecting topics from the Multi-Disciplinary Learning Unit, their performances addressed two major ecological issues in Mumbai: habitat loss due to deforestation and flooding caused by poor waste management. These activities leave a lasting impression, promote change, and connect real-world issues with the scripts.

The series of plays, Mumbai Saga: Stories of Resilience and Survival, introduced students to vulnerable communities in Mumbai, such as the Warli, Koli, and sanitation workers. Performing these characters helped students step into the shoes of these communities, developing empathy and understanding their perspectives.

High school: A focus on perspective taking

Equity, diversity, and inclusion are multidimensional and require an intersectional approach. Social exclusion often stems from age, gender, disability, religion, socioeconomic status, and caste. These concepts should be introduced early, as changing mindsets in adulthood is challenging. GSoM addresses these layers by empowering children with learning challenges through academics, therapies, and life skills training. Performing arts further address other layers of inclusion, helping students confront biases in a safe space.

The high school performances explored themes of kindness, gender stereotypes, polarized perspectives, and hierarchy. The themes for the three performances – growing up, gender identity, and good vs. evil – encouraged students to bring their experiences into the productions and view situations from diverse perspectives. One performance was a classic satire on fairytales and the other two plays were inspired by real-life experiences, promoting self-expression and confidence.

The casting process challenged students' biases and comfort zones, with non-hierarchical casting and students playing characters with different views and experiences. This safe space, created by the Performing Arts team and homeroom teachers, encouraged discussions on rigidity and fluidity in mindsets. For instance, the play “Gender in a Box” directly addresses gender stereotypes such as “boys don’t cry,” “girls don’t play video games,” “boys don’t cook,” and “girls should know how to cook.” It also explores bolder conventions of gendered clothing and makeup. The costumes were a small attempt to break the stereotype of associating pink with femininity and blue with masculinity by having male performers wear pink and female performers wear blue t-shirts. Since then, the learners have embraced this and wear those t-shirts regularly.

The play “Fantasyland vs. The Big Bad Wolf” uses satire and exaggeration to question black-and-white views. As the title suggests, the Big Bad Wolf is on trial for his actions in the stories The Three Little Pigs and The Little Red Riding Hood. The twist in this courtroom drama is that Mowgli from The Jungle Book defends the wolf, highlighting the biases humans have towards someone who looks “different.”

The play “Teenhood” highlights the challenges of hierarchies through ensemble theatre, where there is no hierarchy among the actors. This style places the ensemble in the lead, as opposed to the conventional vertical hierarchy of lead actors, supporting actors, and the chorus.

Differentiation and scaffolding

As the “Arts, Music, Dance and Theatre” position paper published by the National Council of Educational Research and Training states, “Drama has an immense potential to break through stereotypes about what a disabled child can or cannot do.” The students’ individual needs, strengths, and talents are at the forefront while creating these performances. Scripts were edited to suit the linguistic skills of the groups with the help of speech therapists. The entire script was given to the students, not just their parts, to build awareness of the overall performance. Scripts included reference images highlighting the core of each scene and colour-coded highlights for sound cues. Individual copies were further formatted for each learner, emphasizing their dialogues and stage directions.

Initial rehearsals followed the “I Do, We Do, You Do” model, where teachers first enacted the scenes for the students, then performed the scenes together with the students, and finally had the students perform independently. Movement, especially for creating hand shadow puppets for lower school students, was supported by occupational therapists. The community of parents and caregivers plays a vital role in the process. Customized videos and audio recordings for movement and vocal warm-ups were shared with parents and caregivers to support the students at home.

A journey towards co-creation

The productions for the academic year 2023-24 focused on building student agency, while the 2024-25 productions advanced this concept through co-creation. In youth theatre, co-creation involves participants actively engaging in brainstorming and decision-making processes under the mentorship of facilitators, who are also part of the ensemble. Although the ultimate goal is a full-fledged theatre production, the process remains central. Each year, this process extends beyond the drama class through collaborations with other departments, leading to holistic learning.

Creativity allows individuals with disabilities to express themselves in ways that are most suitable for them. Performing arts offer multiple opportunities for both verbal and non-verbal expression. This approach provides role models for other people with disabilities and fosters inclusion among non-disabled individuals through visibility. By doing so, our students can create their own space and invite others into their world.

References

  • ARTS, MUSIC, DANCE AND THEATRE, Position Paper, National Focus Group, National Council of Educational Research and Training
  • Drama and the Education of Young People with Special Needs by Andy Kempe, Key Concepts in Theatre/Drama Education

* Professor, Drama in Education, Reading University, UK

Abhishek Panchal is Vice-Principal and Arts Programme Lead, The Gateway School of Mumbai. He can be reached at apanchal@gatewayschoolmumbai.org.

Nishna Mehta is an Independent Theatre Maker, Arts Manager and Researcher; Performing Arts Programme Consultant, The Gateway School of Mumbai, She can be reached at nishnamehta@gatewayschoolmumbai.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Abhishek Panchal and Nishna Mehta
At Gateway School of Mumbai, performing arts provide children with learning challenges to build skills, empathy, and agency. Abhishek Panchal and Nishna Mehta write about how theatre becomes a tool for inclusion.

item.title: Abhas and the ‘factor’ story
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anil Kumar Patnaik

“Are you ready for the story?” asked Abhas.

“Yesss…” came the reply from his students.

Abhas had promised to tell his students an interesting ‘factor’ story if he got a substitute class.

“Sir, it’s been 10 days! We’ve been waiting for this,” said Prapti.

“You also said you’d ask questions at the end of the story. We’ll try that out too sir,” said Prit.

“The first correct answer will be awarded a Reynolds pen*,” said Abhas.

“Please go ahead with the story, sir. We are desperate, and the bell will ring soon,” beseeched Sohan.

“Okay, okay. This happened a few years ago...,” and so Abhas started.

The story

I was assigned the official task of finding the details of family members of around 100 houses. I had to collect details like name, age, house number, Aadhaar number, etc. Every day, I used to cover 20 houses.

One day, I rang the bell to a house and a lady opened the door. I introduced myself and told her why I had come. She invited me inside her home. I had a feeling I knew her. She brought a glass of water, and suddenly her name sprang from my mouth, “Jhumu?”

She smiled and asked, “Abhas?”

I said, “Yes! It’s a pleasant surprise. How come you are here? And after such a long time that too!”

“My husband got transferred to this place six months ago, and so here I am…”

“And your job?”

“I’m currently focusing on my kids. Once they’re a bit more independent, I’ll get back to work.”

I vividly remembered she was the topper of our batch and popular for her culinary skills. Notwithstanding my refusal, she offered home-baked cake and a cup of masala tea. When you meet old friends, you forget the time, and she had missed our reunions over the years.

Before I realized it, it was time for me to leave, so I asked her for the family details and inquired about the names and ages of her kids.

She started in her usual style of playing with numbers.

“Their names all start with P. You figure out their ages and I’ll tell you their names.”


“Oh God! But how will I know?”

She replied smugly, “Don’t worry, I’ll give you a hint. You are an expert in solving problems and quizzes, right? Figure it out.”

“Okay, tell me then.”

“You know why I quit my job? Because I have to raise three kids. If you multiply their ages, it equals the sum of the digits of my mobile number, which you’ve saved now,” said Jhumu.

I checked and found the sum of the digits of her mobile number to be 72. “That’s not sufficient,” I said. “Give me another hint.”

“Okay, the sum of their ages is my house number,” she replied with a broad smile.

I jumped up from the sofa and went out to see the house number. With hands akimbo, I came back inside and said, “That’s not sufficient. One last hint, please.”

“Hint? Oh no! My eldest one will be returning from coaching. I have to prepare fresh juice for him,” she said.

I said, “Thank you, Jhumu. It’s amazing to reminisce about the old days and spend time with an intelligent friend like you.”

“It’s Parth, Prakriti, and Pratisha,” she answered.

“Take care, Abhas.”

“Convey my regards to Mr. Ghosh and blessings to the kids. I’ll surely meet them sometime.” And I left her house.

Back to the class

My students were gaping at me.

“This is the story, and my question is – can you tell the ages of the three kids and the house number?”

For the next 20 minutes, they were all engrossed in thoughts, working with paper and pencil to find all the factors of 72 and the three numbers.

Suddenly, Aan shouted, “Sir, I got the answer – it’s 36, 2, and 1!” he exclaimed, punching the air.

Rajshri got annoyed and quipped, “Come on, buddy… don’t be silly! How can sir’s friend have a 36-year-old son?”

Everyone laughed and focused again. The bell rang, much to the dismay of the students.

“Okay, kids…. Try it out. If you don’t get the answer, I’ll let you know in the next class.”

As he was about to leave, Prit and Sohan came to Abhas and in a low voice asked, “Sir, just one hint we need. Are Prakriti and Pratisha twins?”

Abhas stopped, beamed radiantly, and said, “Next class, I will explain, and you both will get Reynolds pens.”

Prit and Sohan jumped ecstatically and entered the class. The very next class, Abhas awarded Prit and Sohan with Reynolds pens and explained the details of the story before getting back to the syllabus.

The explanation

The sum of the digits of Jhumu’s cell number is 72, which has to be a product of three numbers. Here are the possible combinations and their sums:

Since there are so many outcomes, Abhas asked for one more hint, but Jhumu cleverly said that the sum of their ages is her house number. Abhas came out to see the house number. If it were any other number, he would have got the answer, but the house number was 14, which has two combinations:

72=6x6x2                                            6+6+2 =14

72=8x3x3                                            8+3+3 =14

He asked for another clue, and Jhumu replied, “My eldest one will be returning from coaching.” Abhas got the answer: 8+3+3, as her younger kids were twins.

In his class too, Sohan and Prit got the clue as they asked whether Prakriti and Pratisha were twins.

Abhas is continuing his journey of unfolding creativity and critical thinking in his classrooms, and moving closer to removing the two large horns that students see on a mathematics teacher’s head.

[*It’s Abhas’s idiosyncrasy to award a student with a Reynolds pen for any out-of-the-box performance. He chooses Reynolds because he had secured a rank in his graduation, writing with a Reynolds.]

This article was first published in the book Seven Mathematical Stories by Anil Kumar Patnaik. It has been published here with permission from the author.

Anil Kumar Patnaik is an award-winning teacher, an exemplary trainer and an orator, fluent in English, Hindi, Odia, and Telugu. He is passionate about improving students learning and creating an effective educational environment by blending creativity and storytelling. He brings out the latent potential of individuals by focusing on people skills. He is a CBSE resource person for the professional development of teachers. A part of the creative team of CBSE, he has created the popular module “Educating Parents about Education”. He can be reached at anilpatnaik8@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anil Kumar Patnaik
Through the story of Abhas’ math classroom, Anil Kumar Patnaik shares how teaching math in interesting ways can enhance students’ creativity and critical thinking.

item.title: Federalism in the Indian Constitution
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: I have been thinking about our conversation on the risk of concentration of power and how the Constitution takes care of it by the separation of powers between the legislature, judiciary, and executive. Another aspect is that individual state governments have the power of administration within their own states and cannot be controlled by the central government. I wonder how this was thought about when the Constitution was drafted. What was their rationale in doing this?

Anubhuti: Yes, it is complicated by the fact that for centuries what we know today as India was many small and large kingdoms. There are periods of time when these were consolidated into empires either by conquest or treaties; but most of them never ruled the entire Indian subcontinent. The Magadha, the Mauryas, and later the Mughals ruled most of what we know as India, but that too covered most of the north of India and lasted a few centuries. They kept moving down south, but there still were many fragments even during the British times. No wonder then that during the time of independence from the British we were numerous princely states, some presidencies, and provinces directly administered by the British government. All of them continued to be sovereign, even though only to some extent. Vallabhbhai Patel, the first home minister, had the tough job of integrating all of them into the Indian Union.

Ira: Like a dear friend says, everything has a history. This process of power sharing had started much before. There were three phases during the British rule – first when the British government dissolved the East India Company and took over the administration of India through the Indian Civil Services in 1858; second with the Montague-Chelmsford Reforms (Government of India Act, 1919), which created a bicameral legislature and increased the participation of Indians in the administration, and finally the Government of India Act in 1935, which established an all-India federation and awarded provincial autonomy.

Of course, the Indian political parties were unhappy with the limited autonomy awarded by the Government of India Act, because the British retained key portfolios like defence and foreign affairs. What was the rationale behind federalism in the Indian Constitution?

Anubhuti: Mainly it has been the consideration that India is a large nation, and different provinces have a history of being autonomous even if partially. The Constituent Assembly was also concerned that there should not be a risk of power being concentrated in a central government. I like what T. T. Krishnamachari said during the debates in the Constituent Assembly. He said, the criterion for whether we are federal or not should be based on three conditions, of which the third is “that the activity of the State must not be completely circumscribed by orders handed down for execution by the superior unit.”[i] He clarified that if power should not be completely circumscribed, it is implied that some power is bound to be controlled by federal authorities. I think it is brilliant how he articulated what we usually call checks and balances. State governments have full power in some areas but are controlled by the central government in other areas. For example, states have full authority in trade and commerce; they can engage directly with even foreign companies and governments, but the nation’s budget is managed by the centre.

Ira: That is why we have these three lists – State list, Union list, and Concurrent list – defined in the Constitution. It is interesting to see these lists in the Constitution. It is a carefully made list of numerous areas which will be dictated by the central government. For instance, I found it amusing that apart from banking and defence, railways are managed by the centre. India’s dependence on the railways to travel is articulated so clearly and strongly here. Of course, the Constitution itself and amendments made to it are also the duty of the legislature. States have authority over theatre and dramatic performances and for cinema; but cinematic exhibitions are controlled by the centre through the CBFC (Central Board for Film Certification).

Anubhuti: Education is an interesting example in the concurrent list. The central government drafts the education policy and curriculum framework, but the states have the right to customize the policy and curriculum to suit their context. I guess the worry here was not so much the risk of concentration of power, as it was about familiarity of cultural context, which is exemplified by language in today’s India.

Ira: Isn’t it fascinating that the Constituent Assembly thought through all this, even though Indian states were not organized based on language. Linguistic reorganization of states happened in 1956, a few years after the Constitution was drafted. But then the first Indian education policy was not out until 1966, so it came late enough that it could be implemented in the reorganized India.

What is the difference between decentralization and distribution of power? I see huge similarities in them. The education example seems similar to decentralization, rather than an example of federalism.

Anubhuti: They are very different – both in purpose and in the way they are implemented. Decentralization is to provide autonomy (not freedom) to the smallest unit of the nation so that policies and law can be efficiently implemented. Take the local self-government as an example. They don’t create policies or interpret them the way they want. They implement policies the way they have been articulated and customize it to suit the immediate needs of the small unit be it a district, taluka, or village panchayat. By the way, this level of autonomy was also implemented as late as 1992 through the 73rd amendment of the Constitution. Whereas federalism allows states to interpret and customize the policy.

This reminds me of the difference of opinion between Gandhi and Ambedkar, with respect to federalism. Gandhi had this imagination of Hind Swaraj in which every village is a self-sufficient unit, with little or no control from higher forms of government – state or central. His argument was unfamiliarity and local autonomy. Whereas, Ambedkar was a strong critic of this model of governance. He believed that villages were the sink of localism and a den of ignorance[ii], and that a higher form of government should control them to ensure they function the way they ought to. He strongly supported the current model of state level federalism.

Ira: I am still thinking of the historical nature of federalism. During the British times, we had presidencies they had formed, numerous kingdoms, and princely states; also some parts of India like Goa and Pondicherry were not in their control. For example, princely states were given more autonomy since they had sovereignty and the British controlled them only through treaties. During independence in 1947, there were around 555 princely states which covered almost 50% of the Indian geography and 28% of the population. Consider Mysore, Hyderabad, Jammu and Kashmir, and many Rajput states. How did the first Indian government even manage all of them?

Anubhuti: Vallabhbhai Patel and V. P. Menon had a tough job on their hands. They spent a lot of effort convincing the sovereigns of the princely states of the advantages of becoming a part of India – mainly because most of them were geographically surrounded by India. The negotiations were done through requesting them to give up defence, communications and foreign affairs, and awarding them privy purses – an amount of money given to the princes from a consolidated fund of India to meet the expenses of their families and monarchical traditions. This privy purse was coded in the Constitution in Article 291, which was removed through an amendment only in 1971.

Seeing the map of India in 1951 and today is very revealing of this transition of power.

Ira: Coming back to federalism, how is it actually coded in the Constitution? Particularly the exceptional situations when the centre can circumscribe the state government.

Anubhuti: Most of it is embedded in particular articles and in Part 5 where the functioning of the union government is written, Part 6 where the functioning of states, and Part 8 which contains how union territories are to be governed. The Seventh Schedule contains the lists of responsibilities of the union, state, and those which are concurrent – both union and states.

Situations when the union government can exercise control are documented in the Directive Principles of State Policy. Here too the Constitution takes a very careful approach to ensure central control cannot be permanent. Article 249 says that the union government can make laws related to the goods and services tax or any matter in the state list ONLY if they have two-thirds majority and this law can be in force only for a year.

Ira: Wow! That is impressive. It is one thing to say we adopted federalism to ensure power is not concentrated in one space and to respect the linguistic and cultural diversity in India. But on the other hand, it is not easy to adopt this kind of federalism unlike say in the United States, which is more strongly federal than us.

Anubhuti: The Constituent Assembly debated this at length – whether to adopt a presidential form of government or parliamentary, whether to mirror their form of federalism or create a new form. The unanimous decision was to borrow the core idea from the USA, but to adapt it to suit the Indian context.

Ira: Our Constitution is rather unique. This is an altogether different type of democracy, not comparable with others. It pushes us to think hard and learn to be a democratic civic culture. Like Madhav Khosla said, ‘our constitution is a pedagogical tool to force us to learn what democracy really is.’

Further reading

  1. “The story of the Integration of the Indian States” by V. P. Menon.
  2. https://www.india.gov.in/my-government/constitution-india
  3. https://www.constitutionofindia.net/

[i] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/25-nov-1949/#130212

[ii] How did B. R. Ambedkar and M. K. Gandhi imagine Indian Federalism? https://www.constitutionofindia.net/podcast/the-constitutionofindia-net-podcast/how-did-b-r-ambedkar-and-m-k-gandhi-imagine-indian-federalism/

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Why does India have both central and state powers? Ira and Anubhuti discuss the historical roots beneath India’s unique federal structure.

item.title: Unpacking Episode 2 of Adolescence
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Simran Luthra

Adolescence, a four-part crime drama,was released on Netflix on 13 March 2025. In the weeks following, it has become one of the most watched and debated shows worldwide. You have likely watched it or encountered some description or discussion of the brilliantly made show.

The plotline revolves around the arrest of a 13-year-old boy, Jamie, who is the prime suspect in the murder of a girl, Katie, from his school. The drama is not crafted as a who-dunit, as CCTV footage clearly shows Jamie stabbing Katie to death. Instead, the makers are more keen on exploring the motive behind the murder. Investigations reveal the bullying of Jamie on social media at the hands of peers, which included Katie. This involved calling him an ‘incel’ (short for ‘involuntary celibate’) and ‘ugly’. In an attempt to restore his self-esteem, Jamie decides to ask Katie out when he believes her to be at a low point. Her refusal is the trigger for his violence against her, resulting in her death.

Critics have lauded the series for its technical craftsmanship. The impeccable writing, the brilliant performances by the actors, and the incredible camerawork, in particular, have impressed audiences everywhere. However, beyond that, the show’s themes have struck a deep chord with parents and invested adults everywhere: the black box that is social media usage by young adults and its consequent impact, as well as the discourses of misogyny and masculine ideals that young adults are exposed to. It has highlighted how disconnected parents and teachers are from the world of adolescents. A nerve has been hit because it is all too close to reality.

Much attention has been paid to episodes 3 (focusing on the interaction between Jamie and the psychiatrist) and 4 (the concluding episode focusing on Jamie’s family and the parents’ guilt). Episode 2, set in the school, is the lowest-rated of all four. It is, I believe, an episode that lends itself to rich analysis and reflection of schooling.

In this article, I attempt a close reading of the episode. I try to explore what the makers provoke us to reflect on with regard to the place of school in society and the role and condition of teachers in their contribution and response to the tragedy that the immensely well-crafted series showcases.

A deep dive into Episode 2…

The episode opens with a photograph showing the victim, Katie, and her young, cherubic face. This is at the site of a temporary memorial that has been set up outside the school for her. This is where the two investigating officers, DI Bascombe and DS Frank, are waiting to be led into the school. Mrs. Fenumore, a teacher from lower school, is in charge of taking the police around the school for investigation. She takes the pair into the school campus. The rolling camera shows the viewer different scenes in the school. We navigate classrooms, hallways, and the dining area. We meet students individually, in pairs, and groups. Given that the series is only four episodes long, the makers have exercised immense economy. Each interaction is hence, noteworthy and significant.

How the teachers expect the students to feel versus how the students are shown to be feeling

Right at the start, Mrs. Fenumore, leading the police officers in, remarks, “Everyone might be a bit raw.” Given that a student from this very school has been killed by another student, this is to be expected. However, most of the children the camera encounters do not seem to be directly impacted by the tragedy. Does this mean they are indifferent? Or is it that the news has not sunk in? Or is it that what we encounter as indiscipline in the school on this day is the children acting out as a coping mechanism? There are no clear answers. Perhaps there can be no singular answers either. However, the indifference of most of the children and the fact that business-as-usual has resumed in school so soon are disturbing at a subliminal level.

Safety in the school

One of the initial scenes follows a conversation between two teachers, Mrs. Bailey and Derek. Derek shares that he has received calls from 14 parents asking if the school was safe. Typical of school managements, he adds that the incident did not even take place on school property. The fact that both children involved in the tragic incident knew each other from school is not acknowledged. He shares that the parents were talking about metal detectors and security guards. Mrs. Bailey also shares that one of the parents suggested opening a grief support room for students. Derek asks a rhetorical question at this point: “We’re security guards and social workers now, are we?” indicating the sort of intense labour expected from teachers and the myriad functions society expects schools to play.

Two notions of safety are discussed in this exchange: physical and emotional. However, it is the latter that is the truly important one, even though, because of the instance of knife violence, it may seem that physical safety is the problem.

The makers indicate that the real danger lies not in weapons, for which metal detectors and security guards are needed. It is the mobile phones that all students carry. Teachers at various points in the episode are shown to be shouting at students to put their phones away, but to no avail. This indicates the pervasive dangers of phones and social media, particularly for young minds. The massive response to the show has, in fact, encouraged the writers to call for a social media ban for children under the age of 16. Lawmakers in the UK are currently discussing this issue in parliament.

The episode continuously highlights the lack of emotional regulation among the school children, the prevalence and, in fact, normalization of bullying. Just before the previously described scene, we meet Adam. He is the son of Officer Bascombe, who is shown to be at the receiving end of mass bullying. He is shown to be a lone figure with no friends. Classmates dump food wrappers on his plate in the dining hall. A classmate of his, Fredo, charges at him aggressively, demanding £1.20. A teacher intervenes. The teacher asks Fredo to leave Adam alone but argues logically with Fredo, saying he does not need the money as he is on free dinners. The scene suggests that the bullying Adam faces is routine and has become an accepted part of the school culture. Such incidents seem to be handled reactively and superficially.

This preoccupation with the superficial is highlighted again when, immediately after expressing her concern about the impact of the tragic incident, Mrs. Bailey reprimands two boys passing by for their untucked shirts. The boys apologize to her, but once she is out of earshot, they swear and remark, “What the ***** she on about?” So much of what school authorities and teachers are concerned about is peripheral and unimportant, while what truly matters is the emotional health of the children.

The place of emotions

The episode subtly keeps highlighting the primacy of emotions. Bascombe’s investigation strategy also focuses on emotions. He asks Jamie’s friend Ryan if Jamie was happy or sad on the night of the murder. Ryan replies, saying he was normal. However, Bascombe’s line of inquiry is also problematic as it is too black and white. He is taught how nuanced emotions are when his son, Adam, enlightens him about the meanings of emoticons used on social media. Bascome comments, “It’s just hard to believe, like all that from two symbols”, indicating how nuanced an emotional world, children, especially adolescents, inhabit. It is a fallacy to only look for binaries such as ‘happy’ or ‘sad’ in a world where a comment on an Instagram post that looks like a compliment can mean the very opposite.

Foregrounding ‘adolescence’

By calling the show Adolescence, perhaps the makers also want to remind us of the criticality of this phase of human life. It is a phase marked by significant physical, psychological, and social transitions. It is characterized by rapid growth, hormonal fluctuations, and brain development. The prefrontal cortex, responsible for higher-level cognitive functions like planning, impulse control, and decision-making, undergoes major changes during adolescence. The song at the end of the episode summarizes it best: “Lest we forget how fragile we are.” Indeed, humans, and particularly adolescents, are fragile creatures who need to be handled with utmost care.

While the plot revolves around the crime committed by Jamie, Episode 2 shows us other adolescents who are also high-strung and act impulsively. Jade, Katie’s best friend, is called in for interrogation. Understandably, she is unable to cooperate with the police as she feels as though they are questioning Katie’s character and blaming Katie. However, during a fire drill, we see Jade physically assaulting Jamie’s friend, Ryan, who she believes is indirectly responsible for her friend’s death. Later, she is shown to repent her violence. She is fearful of the consequences only after the surge of emotions has settled. Had there not been people around to break them up, could Jade’s violence against Ryan have been worse? We do not know. The point is that adolescence is a time when people are highly sensitive and emotional. Unless we engage with young people in a manner that ensures their emotional and mental wellbeing, there is always the risk of emotions running amok.

Need for invested adults (teachers and parents) to be more clued in to the larger world

Schools are not islands. While they may seem to be isolated spaces meant for formal education, they are very much a part of the societal continuum. Schools must be spaces that are aware of and critically engage with the world outside. It is crucial for teachers to be well-informed of realities that may impact children and teens. Mrs. Fenumore, for instance, expresses her lack of awareness regarding ‘incel culture’. When Detective Frank explains it, she remarks that she has heard the boys talking about Andrew Tate. A bit of curiosity might have led Mrs. Fenumore to uncover the dangers posed by Andrew Tate and all that he represents.

Bascombe expresses his surprise at this in his interaction with Adam, saying that, of course, 13-year-old boys would necessarily be celibate. He is unable to comprehend the relevance of any talk of ‘involuntary celibates’ among teen boys. Children, however, are little people. They are far more interested in the larger world and the world of adults than adults realize. Their interpretation of adult-world topics can also be heavily distorted. Further, this interest can become vulnerable and misguided if the world they inhabit ceases to remain engaging and meaningful. It is imperative for adults to be socially aware and vigilant about ideas accessible to younger people.

A contrasting parenting style

The most significant relationship for a child is with their parent(s). Hence, even while we are immersed in the world of the school, which shows students and teachers, one parent-child relationship is foregrounded: that of DI Bascombe and his son, Adam. At the start of the episode, we see Bascombe messaging his son, promising him that he would leave him alone. Bascombe is perhaps aware that teenagers like to keep a distance from their parents, especially when at school. At first, this could seem blameworthy. This is because we see Adam being bullied by various children in the dining hall. However, through the episode, we see Bascombe reflecting on himself as a parent. He admits to feeling that he is unsuited to be a parent but also to his love for his son.

I argue that this father-son relationship is juxtaposed with that of the Miller father-son duo. Like Jamie, Adam too is a victim of bullying. Perhaps like Miller, Bascombe too, at the start of the episode, seems to be keeping a distance from his son. The idea that adolescents need space is an important one. However, the balance between providing space and smothering one’s child is a delicate one.

The episode ends with Bascombe taking Adam out for a meal. The father tells the son that he loves him and that he wants to spend time with him. This open expression of love and the invitation to spend time together come after Bascombe is shown reluctant to bother Adam at the start. It shows how the tragedy has perhaps helped Bascombe reassess his parenting style. It also invites all invested adults to rethink their relationships with children. Authoritarianism and disconnection need to be supplanted by friendship and connection.

Teachers in the school

This brief episode focusing on the murder investigation manages to give a sense of the classroom culture and interactions between students and teachers. For the most part, the teachers’ tones and voices sound frustrated and exasperated. Teachers are frequently shown raising their voices at children and reprimanding them. Only one classroom, that of Mr. Curtis’s, stands out in stark contrast as it is quiet and orderly. Mr. Curtis is calm for the most part and tries to connect with students by explaining the purpose of the detectives’ visit to the school (see below image). Although it is important to also note that the classroom has a preponderance of female students, who again are playing their gender roles and are quieter than the boys. This is also Adam’s class and he is once again bullied by Fredo. But this time, Mr. Curtis reprimands Fredo strongly, asking to meet him after class. There is hope of an intervention rather than a superficial engagement like we saw in the dining hall.

Overall, there is an air of busyness, and the school feels chaotic and overwhelming. Yet, at the same time, the students seem bored and disconnected. Perhaps this is intended to reflect the ennui and boredom characteristic of several school spaces which do not meaningfully engage students or teachers. Instead, teachers are constantly trying to ‘manage’ classrooms and discipline children.

Gendered roles are visible among the male and female teachers. The male teachers do most of the disciplining or are at least able to get students to behave better. The female teachers are shown to be engaging in more emotional labour (like Mrs. Bailey talking with Jade) and seem softer overall. They also seem to be disrespected more easily by students, reflecting the larger societal gender dynamic. In an almost triggering scene, Mrs. Fenumore asks a group of students to put away their phones, and a student screams “Shut up!” at her, to which she responds with an “Excuse me?” and continues to walk ahead. The scene also shows the occasional humiliation involved in the work of teachers that is hardly ever spoken of. Such gendered experiences further reinforce stereotypes and maintain the vicious cycle.

Teachers and their experiences are often neglected in research, policy, and even popular culture, but they matter immensely. After all, they are the ones expected to pour from their cups. Whether their cups are empty or full should matter to schools, parents, and society at large. Bascombe tries to speak to Mr. Malik, who is Jamie’s class teacher, to learn more about Jamie. Mr. Malik is hesitant. The dialogue between the two is telling:

Mr. Malik: I’m… I’m just a tutor.

Bascombe: Well, you’re a teacher.

Mr. Malik: Yeah, but I teach history. I don’t see him, you know? I don’t… I wouldn’t know. I mean these kids are ******* impossible, what am I supposed to do? [walks away]

This is not merely about Mr. Malik or about the kind of teacher he is. In the mainstream system, teachers still define their work as teaching subjects and not teaching little humans (like Mr. Malik says, he does not ‘see’ Jamie). The fact is that teachers are overworked and cannot be held responsible for everything. Responsibility for younger generations is a collective one. Mainstream schools function in tandem with mainstream societal discourses and are hence unable to challenge these discourses.

Schools: spaces of oppression or hope?

Towards the end of the episode, DI Bascombe comments that the school feels like a ‘holding pen’. A ‘holding pen’ refers to an area used to confine animals. Bascombe’s comparison is derogatory, but not inaccurate. The series does a masterful job in showing how larger societal mindsets get perpetuated and reach younger generations. It also shows how schools are ineffectual in preventing such damage from happening because in their current form they are wholly unprepared for such realities.

It is important to bear in mind the origins of the modern school, which came up during the industrial revolution to create docile and obedient workers and as a ‘state apparatus’ to instil nationalist fervour among citizens. While these are not explicit aims of schools today, most schools continue to function in this older mould. We have to break out of this mould if schools are to be spaces that do not oppress but liberate. We have to think in terms of more humanistic values and practices that reflect the concerns of our times. It should not matter if boys do not have their shirts tucked in. What is going on in their minds and hearts is what should be of concern.

At one point following the investigators, we see a poster on a soft board that says ‘You are a rainbow of possibilities’. Nothing in the series is extraneous or meaningless. The poster is a poignant reminder of the possibilities that each child holds for who they can be in the world. Such a message stands in stark contrast to the reality of the crime committed and the oppressive space that the school is shown to be. The place where children spend the majority of their time, the place meant to nurture and bring the best out in them, is an opportunity and space wasted in its present form.

What can we do then?

While larger societal changes at the level of policy and controls may be harder to achieve and more elusive, what is immediately within our circle of control and influence are the relationships we have with children as teachers, parents, or invested adults. Building strong human connections within schools, discussing real-world problems with compassion and empathy, not reducing children and their worth to marks, and paying attention to their emotional safety and mental wellbeing are imperative steps that educators and adults need to take. These should take precedence over achievement of learning outcomes because it would enable better learning to also take place.

Schools need to be spaces where teachers and students meaningfully explore education and life. There is an increasing awareness of the importance of life skills as opposed to mastering content through rote. However, it will take intent and effort on the part of all stakeholders to break out of the stranglehold of the older mould of schooling for both teachers and students. Unless that happens we cannot expect to cater to the unprecedented realities of the world we inhabit today.

The show holds up a mirror and shows us how the school is part and an enabler in some senses of the problem and not part of the solution. Instead, schools need to be spaces that transform and do not reproduce the flawed society we live in. Schools too are rainbows of possibility and can choose to be what they become.

Simran Luthra holds a PhD in Education from the School of Education, Tata Institute of Social Sciences (TISS), Mumbai. She also has a Master’s in English from Jadavpur University, Kolkata, and a Master’s in Education from TISS, Mumbai. A former Fulbright Foreign Language Teaching Assistant at Stanford University (2013-14), Simran has over a decade of experience in the field of education. Currently based in Pune, she continues to contribute to education through her research, teaching, and writing. She can be reached at simranluthra@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Simran Luthra
The emotional world of teenagers is fragile and fraught with bullying. Simran Luthra unpacks the second episode of Adolescence which shows a school in the wake of a tragedy.

item.title: Understanding anger
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sujata C

Learning life skills is an essential part of growing up. A project based approach to learning about anger and its management is a proven method that will help in managing this emotion constructively. There is a growing concern among psychologists and teachers regarding increased aggression among children and their violent behaviour. This seems to be driven by factors like external influence, family disturbances, societal pressures, media/social media influence, reduced parental control, etc. There are an alarming number of reports in the media about violent acts being committed, especially by adolescents for very trivial reasons. There is research to show the prevalence of a range of anger issues among Indian adolescents, with some research papers suggesting that anger is affecting between 17.7% and 66.5% of school-going children.

A project on anger is expected to help children understand anger and develop some anger management skills. This project is likely to be different from other projects as it is based on learning a life skill and about understanding the self.

The seeds of anger

Anger is one of the negative emotions that most living beings experience. It is an emotional response to perceived threats, injustice, or frustration. It is a natural response and can even be healthy in certain situations. It is a part of the body’s fight-or-flight response, a survival mechanism that prepares us to face danger or flee from it. Unmet needs, shame, fear, a sense of hopelessness and inadequacy, a mismatch between expectations and reality – all these can make one angry.

How we experience anger

The biology of anger: When we are angry, our bodies undergo several physiological changes, such as an increased heart rate, elevated blood pressure, and a surge of adrenaline. According to traditional Chinese medicine, emotions are connected to various body organs and affect them directly and in different ways. Anger is connected to the liver. In a state of anger, cortisol, the stress hormone gets released and this inflames the liver. The liver stores and processes the anger leading to excessive bile production, which in turn tends to give rise to acidity. Over a period of time, this will lead to liver malfunction, which may have to be addressed with detoxification therapies.

Physical effects of chronic anger

1. Cardiovascular problems: Chronic anger can lead to high blood pressure, heart attacks, and strokes.

2. Weakened immune system: Persistent anger can weaken the immune system, making the body more susceptible to illnesses.

3. Headaches and digestive problems: Anger can also contribute to physical symptoms such as headaches and digestive issues.

4. Inflammation: Anger activates the body’s stress response, releasing cortisol and adrenaline, which can lead to physiological changes in the body including inflammation.

Psychological effects of chronic anger: Hot headed people tend to get angry easily and quickly for every small thing. This can lead to confrontations, conflicts, and strained relationships. They may sometimes do things that they regret later.

Anger is of two types: healthy and unhealthy. Not all anger is bad. Healthy anger is expressed when one wants to protect someone or something or bring about a positive change in a situation. A parent’s anger when disciplining a child is healthy and controlled. Uncontrolled anger that is unreasonable is unhealthy.

Suppressing or controlling emotions is not the best way to handle them and experts say that emotions are best expressed in healthy ways.

Healthy ways of expressing anger

  1. Assertiveness: Expressing anger in a direct, respectful, and honest way, setting limits on what you are willing or not willing to do in a situation.
  2. Problem-solving: Identifying the reasons for anger and coming up with strategies to address the situation.
  3. Communication: Talking to someone you trust or seeking professional help to manage anger.
  4. Physical activity: Engaging in exercise or sports or any other household activity like cleaning to release pent-up energy.

When things go wrong we often tell people to stay strong. To stay strong in negative circumstances means not losing self-control and not succumbing to the urge to yell, threaten, abuse, or adopt any other unhealthy way of expressing anger.

Unhealthy ways of expressing anger

Short-term strategies to deal with situations:

Practicing emotional release is important in anger management. Several activities can be planned to help students understand anger and find ways of releasing it.

Language activity: There is a saying in Hindi “Krodh paap ka baap hai”, which translates to ‘Anger is the father of sins’. Collect sayings by talking to elders in the family and other acquaintances, in languages that you know about anger and discuss them in class.

Poster making: Make posters with some well-known quotations/sayings on anger.

Laughter therapy: Laughter is a mood enhancer. It reduces stress and has a positive effect on the immune system. Teachers can set aside one day in the week to have a laughter yoga class for 10 minutes.

Craft work: Make an anger thermometer using chart paper, markers, and crayons. For degrees of anger use words like calm, irritated, frustrated, angry, and very angry. Colour in the thermometer to show the state of feelings at different levels of anger. Discuss what triggers each level and ways to cool down when the temperature rises.

Role play is a good technique to teach children the right ways of expressing and managing anger. Create scenarios where kids might feel angry, such as someone taking their toy or someone shouting at them.

Have small groups of students act out these scenarios, demonstrating healthy ways to express and manage anger and resolve the situation. It will help them re-enact it in real-life situations.

Art therapy: The element associated with anger is fire. The colour associated with anger is red.

Provide students with materials like paper, markers, and paint, and ask them to draw or create something that represents their anger (abstract shapes, colours, or images).

Afterward, have them write a brief explanation about their artwork.

Meditation and mindfulness: A world exploding with information, YouTube videos and Insta reels are robbing children of the inherent ability for mindfulness. Mindfulness is the awareness of the present moment; everything about the present moment, the field of awareness, the body sensations, the temperature in the room, the brightness of the sun, the movement of the breath, the ambient sounds, calmly acknowledging one’s feelings, good or bad, contributes to mindfulness. Research says that using mindfulness based interventions can help children become self-aware to begin with. This act of reflection and contemplation, over a period of time, may lead to self-mastery and self-regulation.

Yoga: Yoga is also one of the long-term strategies that help in calming the mind. It is a holistic practice that combines and integrates the body, mind, and soul and promotes physical and mental wellness. When one does asanas with their eyes closed, the mind goes inwards, becoming aware of all the sensations in the body, the thoughts, both good and bad that come up. Under a trained yoga teacher, the mind can learn to accept thoughts and experience stillness of the mind.

Raaga therapy: Classical Indian Carnatic music is very useful in healing and used frequently as part of treatments. Raga Sahana is said to be useful in dealing with anger issues.

Pranayama: The power of breath is underestimated. Brahmari pranayama helps in restoring emotional balance. Studies conducted by ayurvedic scholars confirm this.

Journaling: Journaling is a great strategy for self-help and self-improvement. Ask students to write down everything they feel and what they do when they are angry. This will help them become aware of how they are expressing their anger and also of their triggers. For example:

1. External events: Situations such as traffic jams, long queues, or arguments with others can provoke anger.

2. Internal events: Personal problems, memories of past events, or worrying about future events can also lead to feelings of anger.

Gratitude journaling works even better. It helps in self-reflection and personal growth. A journal entry might read like this: I am feeling very upset and angry. Things didn’t go right for me today. Why does this have to happen with me all the time? I feel like doing something nasty. Don’t ask me to forgive and all that. I’m so mad at so and so. But wait, there are many other things that did go right for me today. Let me focus on what I have rather than what I don’t. I had a healthy breakfast. My mother packed my favourite snack in my lunch box, the sky was blue in the morning, so on and so forth.

Thinking in a positive way helps the mind move away from the angry feelings. When distanced from difficult emotions, the stress gets released. Thinking in a positive way helps in improving the mood. But negative thinking comes easily to the brain, whereas positive thinking takes some effort. With a little initial practise, it gets easier. Gratitude journaling regularly helps train the mind to focus on the positive things.

Help your class understand that anger is a natural emotion that everyone experiences, but it can have significant negative effects if not managed properly. By learning to control anger, we can navigate life’s challenges more effectively and maintain a more balanced and peaceful state of mind.

Sujata C is an independent writer, editor and lifelong learner. She can be reached at sujata117@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sujata C
Anger isn’t always bad. And children need to be taught how to understand, express, and transform their anger in a constructive manner. Sujata C shares some creative tools.

item.title: Walking the talk
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

It was time for annual stocktaking in school. The principal oversaw this routine every year, and as she briskly went around checking if all items were being examined, she paused at the school library. Piles of unused books were being dusted off from the shelves and scrutinized against the booklist.

“I didn’t realize we had so many books!” she exclaimed. “We seem to look at them only during this annual activity! Why don’t they get read more often?”

That question stayed with her even as she ran through her more-than-usually packed day at the end of the academic year.

Weren’t books meant to be read? What could be done about this? How could she make reading an integral part of the school curriculum?

Slowly, she began to think of ways to create a reading culture among her students, at least the 8-12 year-olds to begin with.

I happened to speak to the principal as she started planning her action research on the following problem: To develop strong reading habits (as part of the curriculum) in 8-12 year-olds.

Almost as soon as we began to chat, she realized that she would not be in a position to conduct the action research (AR) on her own, bogged down as she was by her administrative duties. There simply wouldn’t be enough time, she acknowledged. How could she therefore enthuse a core group of (about eight) teachers to undertake this action research? To do that, she would have to first infuse her teachers with a love for reading. Not that there weren’t avid readers in her teaching faculty – there were a few. But by and large, her teachers restricted themselves to reading the textbooks so as to transact the lessons in class.

Then came the question: How would her engagement with the teachers differ from that with students, since she would have done the latter if she conducted AR for the stated problem?

Now she realized that she would have to reframe the AR problem thus: To enthuse teachers to cultivate a reading culture in 8-12 year-old students in the school.

As we got deeper into the discussion, I casually asked her about her own reading habits.

“Were you always a good reader, from childhood?”

“I don’t read much,” she admitted. “I read newspaper articles, and whatever is required for a given task. Easy reading I can always do. But I don’t read anything apart from that. No books …”

I then asked her why this was so. Was it lack of access? Lack of time? She honestly admitted that she had never really developed the habit herself. “I am unable to complete whatever I start reading…I buy lots of books, but somehow I never get around to finish reading what I start …”

This made both of us sit up. Surely if she could get to the root of her own inner resistance to reading regularly, she would be better equipped to conduct this sort of AR?

The principal saw how much her AR problem had shifted. It now seemed far more feasible. And this got her very excited. She began devoting an hour a day to reading – from the very next day.

The best part of this story is that we had this conversation more than three years ago.

I last spoke to her a week ago: and would you believe it, she has not missed a single day since! She has continued the practice of devoting an hour every day to reading ever since that day; in short, she has formed a new habit in her middle age!

We often stress the importance of a teacher/parent modelling the behaviour that they wish to see in their students/children.

If a principal models the behaviour that she wishes to see in her teachers, aren’t the chances of a ripple effect in the entire school far higher?

Watch the video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CUdphAdOVhA&t=50s

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What began as a routine library check led to a powerful personal transformation for a school principal writes Neeraja Raghavan.

item.title: Global mindset in teachers: a perspective for math educators
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

"Culture refers to a group or community with which we share common experiences that shape the way we understand the world. It includes groups that we are born into, such as gender, race, national origin, class, or religion. It can also include groups we join or become part of," says Axner (n.d.). The worldview that we develop is shaped by the cultures we are born into and those that we encounter throughout our lives.

The group I was born into

I was born into a society steeped in tradition and collectivism, where the role of women was often narrowly defined by the family structure. My upbringing as a Hindu woman was guided by a set of values that reflected the cultural norms of my community. In this environment, a girl's worth was often seen through the lens of her future as a wife and daughter-in-law. My mother, loving yet practical, focused on teaching me how to run a household, be self-sufficient, and ultimately, prepare for a life where my main role would be to serve the family I would eventually marry into.

As a child, I always had an inherent inclination toward individuality, and I struggled to reconcile with the cultural values I was taught. While my family instilled in me values of hard work, sincerity, and collectivism, I found myself constantly questioning the limited scope of my future as a woman.

Reflection questions
  1. What cultural expectations shaped your early views on education and teaching?
  2. How has your personal journey influenced your approach to fostering independence in students?
  3. What aspects of collectivist values do you see as beneficial in the classroom?

The group I moved to

The first significant shift in my worldview came when I discovered progressive education through the Sri Aurobindo Ashram schools in New Delhi. For the first time, I encountered a space where both academic learning and spiritual growth were intertwined. The teachings of Sri Aurobindo and The Mother helped me see that there was more to education than just rote memorization or societal expectations.

It was in these schools that I first encountered a model of education that emphasized the holistic development of students – not just academic achievements, but personal growth, self-reflection, and the cultivation of a sense of purpose. This was the space where I found my calling as an educator.

Reflection questions

  1. How has exposure to different educational philosophies influenced your teaching methods?
  2. What role does personal growth play in your approach to math education?
  3. How can you create a classroom environment that fosters both academic and emotional intelligence?

Present time: a global mindset in math education

Today, my worldview is shaped by a global mindset that allows me to embrace diversity in all its forms. This mindset deeply influences my approach to teaching math. Mathematics is often considered a universal language, yet the way it is taught varies widely across cultures. A global mindset in math education means recognizing different approaches to problem-solving, valuing diverse mathematical traditions, and fostering an inclusive classroom where all students feel capable of engaging with numbers.

For math teachers, adopting a global mindset means:

  • Encouraging students to explore mathematical concepts through multiple cultural perspectives.
  • Emphasizing problem-solving strategies from different countries and traditions.
  • Using real-world examples that connect math to global issues such as climate change, financial literacy, and data representation.
  • Promoting critical thinking by allowing students to question and analyze the reasoning behind mathematical methods rather than simply memorizing formulas.
Reflection questions
  1. How does your own cultural background influence the way you teach mathematics?
  2. Have you explored mathematical problem-solving approaches from different cultures in your classroom? If not, how can you start?
  3. How can you help your students see math as a tool for understanding and solving global issues?

Conclusion

A worldview, as Abi-Hashem (n.d.) notes, can be generic or specific, and it can reflect either a global perspective or a deep connection to local heritage. The key is to remain open to both, to learn from one’s roots, and to engage with the broader world with a spirit of curiosity and humility. Math educators, in particular, have a unique opportunity to use their subject as a bridge between cultures, fostering a deeper understanding of both numbers and the world they represent.

Reference

Axner, D. (1993). The Community leadership project curriculum. Pomfret, CT: Topsfield Foundation.

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Math is a universal language, but how we teach it isn’t. Monica Kochar writes about how a global mindset can make all the difference.

item.title: Teacher autonomy – it’s time we claim what is rightfully ours!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

Recently, at a family wedding, I happened to catch up with a long-lost cousin who has been working on a very fascinating project. The company he works at in America (DoorDash, a food delivery app) is in the pilot phase of creating autonomous robots which are equipped to deliver food within demarcated areas. This robot can navigate traffic, both vehicular and human, follow traffic lights, know when to pull over (police car, ambulance, etc.) and when not to (fascinated passerby trying to take a selfie!). They are building the intelligence of this robot in a way that the input it keeps receiving from engaging with the world helps it build future intelligence for itself to deal with uncertainties it may face. The attempt is to make this robot autonomous with little dependency on human intervention.

This got me thinking. Isn’t this how we have always ‘learned’? Engage with the world around us. Understand it through exploration, discovery, and experimentation. And finally, use all the inputs from these engagements to keep engaging with other phenomena around you. It’s an ongoing way of being. It has always been! Every newborn child starts off their life on the planet this way. Every primary school textbook tells us how the early man did it! Yet, we fail to recognize the importance of autonomous learning systems in education. How are we to build our own learning systems and develop our innate intelligence if we are to sit inside classrooms, cut off from the world and its complexities that we are supposed to be engaging with?

Instead, we are kept busy (more than we should be) engaging with a curriculum that is often disconnected from the ongoings of the world, and dealing with superficial assessments that give you a fallacious sense of what learning looks like. This finally results in a distorted divide between “successful” learners with an inflated pride, and “failed” learners with low self-esteem; both, having built some level of trauma-bond with the idea of ‘learning’. In all of this, the teacher is left feeling exhausted, burnt-out, and completely confused about what their role has been in this process of learning for the child.

I write from the point of view of both the child learner as well as the teacher. They engage with each other as part of a system, where the primary objective is to develop learning skills and systems of intelligence within ourselves, which will help us engage with the world and its uncertain future. Pretty much what DoorDash is doing with their robots right now. However, the difference here is that we seem to have forgotten the importance of autonomy in learning. Education systems the world over have stripped the classroom of all autonomy. Both teacher and learner autonomy.

Small attempts and big successes

However, I am reminded of a bunch of extremely enthusiastic, excited, and bright-eyed government schoolteachers I met in Delhi at a conference I was attending. I have had my years of working in government schools and I can say with confidence that this bunch was an anomaly. They were strategizing on how to ensure teachers, principals, and decision-makers from outside of Delhi got a copy of the Happiness Curriculum they were distributing. I was privileged to receive a copy and have the opportunity to talk with them at length. They explained in detail what the curriculum was about, what the impact has been in class, anecdotes from students, parents, and other teachers. I loved the enthusiasm, and I was curious to know where this intense sense of ownership came from.

Slowly, I understood. The thinkers behind this curriculum valued autonomy. The system of designing and implementing this curriculum was a shared responsibility across educational hierarchies. The teachers I was talking to shared that they had fallen into a slump teaching their subjects in the same manner for several decades but felt reborn when they got to take charge of this curriculum. A Happiness Curriculum Committee was formed, which comprised policy makers, SEL experts, and other government officials, but it also had regular schoolteachers, who were directly responsible for the implementation of this programme. From what I understood, these ‘mentor teachers’ (yes, they got a new name!) were assigned a bunch of schools each and were responsible to ensure the teachers in each of those schools get equipped to deliver this new curriculum. The teachers could contact the mentor teachers directly and they, in turn, had to report directly to the Committee. In addition, they shared, almost with pride, that neither the teacher, nor the mentor teacher had to go through the principal anymore. I remember what one of them said, “It’s like a bypass. It’s more efficient!”

It was magically inspiring for me to see how autonomy can turn around the self-determination of an individual and push them towards a growth mindset with such ease.

Neeraja Raghavan, founder-director of Thinking Teacher and a regular writer for Teacher Plus, recently brought out an excellent book titled A Learning Community of Reflective Teachers. It documents what happens when a group of teachers across the country decide to start an initiative to collaborate and learn together. Through discussions, debates, and action research projects that these teachers lead, the book showcases a solid argument towards a new way forward for teacher development where teachers drive it themselves, with the support of each other, always focussing on the voice of the teacher.

I co-design the Artful Teacher Workshop Programme, which has a strong focus on empowering teachers to build agency and be proactive in reclaiming our autonomy. How? By engaging with ourselves through the arts. By pushing ourselves out of the comfort zones we’ve slipped into. By building a tribe of teachers who meet regularly to learn together. By agreeing to open our classrooms to each other, and be ready to build a space of support, trust, and vulnerability. To make conscious, determined efforts, within the four walls of our classroom, to experiment, play, build confidence, and articulation, allowing ourselves to experience first-hand the joy and freedom that taking ownership offers.

So, when did we become non-thinkers?

Today, we perceive teachers as executers, instead of thinkers. Remember the village masterji? He (unfortunately, always male!) was always a friend, philosopher, and guide to all the villagers, with an expansive vision and an imagination to think beyond the obvious. He set a living example of having a positive relationship with learning, and developing one’s innate intelligence to be able to offer progressive solutions to complex problems that a village would face with changing times.

With the advancement of the colonial system of education, the life of the local community found no reflection in the school’s curriculum. In their view, indigenous knowledge and culture were ‘deficient’ and the school curriculum became totally disassociated from the Indian child’s everyday life. Moreover, the teacher had no say in the selection of knowledge and his low salary and status ensured that he would not exercise any professional autonomy or even have a professional identity. (Krishna Kumar, 1991)

At the brink of independence, when there was a nation-wide Community Development Programme (CDP), the village teacher was fondly remembered again! He (still remained male…we’re talking 1952) played an essential role in the attempt to make their community self-sufficient and self-reliant. This programme gave complete autonomy to the villagers and only acted to ‘assist’ the village in this process. (Publications Division, 1959) I’m not sure how successful this programme was over the decades that followed, but we can take a moment to focus on the fact that at a time when we were finding ways to build a nation, our thinkers having experienced what it meant to finally be free, believed, not in a directed top-down approach, but in a way forward that had autonomy at its core.

Today, as the world is going to war with each other and world leaders (not thinkers) are celebrating blood baths, the ongoing colonial system of education works in their favour, training generation after generation to be obedient and submissive. Perhaps, it’s time for a quiet, gentle revolution to spark off where teachers – the real thinkers – see the four walls of their classroom not as a limitation but as a safe space to ignite thought and thinking within our learners. I’ll end with Paulo Freire, with his skillful ways of showing us which direction to follow: “Reading the world always precedes reading the word, and reading the word implies continually reading the world.” It’s time for education to prioritize this.

References

  • Krishna Kumar, 1991, 2005, Political Agenda of Education, Sage Publications
  • Publications Division, 1959, Village Teacher’s Role in Community Development, Govt of India Press
  • Neeraja Raghavan, 2024, A Learning Community of Reflective Teachers, Routledge India
  • Paulo Freire, 1970, Pedagogy of the Oppressed.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator, and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Can a curriculum make teachers feel reborn? Timira explores how autonomy, not obedience, can transform classrooms, and the people in them.

item.title: Why history is important
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Q and A with Prof. Krishna Kumar

Chintan Girish Modi

Professor Krishna Kumar, who served as the Director of India’s National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) from 2006 to 2010, was among the speakers at the 2025 edition of the Jaipur Literature Festival.

Apart from teaching at Delhi University’s Central Institute of Education from 1981 to 2016, he has written a number of books that would interest teachers, activists, scholars, and policymakers. His work draws attention to the political agenda of education, the role of history textbooks in nation-building as well as fomenting suspicion and antagonism, the challenges that history teachers face from the entertainment industry, and how the teaching of history can contribute to peace in our society when it is done thoughtfully and sensitively.

Here are some excerpts from our conversation on the sidelines of the literature festival.

Your latest book How to Teach History (2023), which addresses school teachers directly, is based on your lectures at the History for Peace conferences in 2018 and 2019. Why do you think it is important for historians to interact with history teachers?

Because history is like no other subject. Unless there is a teacher who mediates between the past and the child, the child will not be able to discover the past in any meaningful, systematic sense. On the teacher falls this very unique and very sensitive responsibility to initiate the child into this world which existed long before his or her birth. A history teacher inducts the child into a human community. We are all creatures of history. And there is no limit to how far back you can go. It depends, to a large extent, on the child’s curiosity. “What happened before I came into this world?” is the question that drives this curiosity. And the job of the teacher is to arouse and nurture that curiosity.

What kind of agency does a history teacher have in the current political environment, where teachers are being reduced to vehicles for delivering curriculum?

I know what you mean but I hate the word ‘delivery’. I mean, that’s the job of a postman and not a teacher. Delivering the curriculum makes it sound as if the curriculum is somehow fixed, dead, and has to be given as a parcel. Instead, we should be talking more about how to make knowledge come alive in the classroom. No matter how it is presented in a textbook, ultimately it is the teacher who makes knowledge come alive for young people. If it is not happening today, that is a different issue.
