But I know that it is happening in many classrooms where teachers are still relating to children through that knowledge.

You have written about how history is taught in India and Pakistan in books like Prejudice and Pride (2001) and Battle for Peace (2007). It has been a while since those books came out. Do you intend to re-assess what the situation is now?

Both these books are still selling. At the Jaipur Literature Festival, I was really pleasantly surprised to see the fourth reprint of one and the third reprint of the other. But I guess books, like cats, have many lives. There is so much more to say about history teaching in India and Pakistan but at the moment I am not returning to that theme. I am working on a book that revolves around the life of a teacher called Surina Behenji. It is being published by Rajkamal Prakashan. It is a work of fiction about a government school teacher. She is posted in Devpur – a town that you can place anywhere in northern India – and then transferred around. It is about her life as she negotiates multiple schools and conflicts in society.

How do you look back at your tenure as the Director of NCERT, and developments in Indian education after the National Curriculum Framework of 2005?

We did our job and very soon you will read a new book of mine, which is a memoir with reflections on our work and what happened later. It will come out next year. The publisher will be decided soon. I have not yet given my manuscript. It is called The Seatbelt Years.

What advice would you give history teachers in India?

Hold on to this very important task of introducing children to the past.

Chintan Girish Modi has an M.Phil. in English Language Education. He has worked with the UNESCO Mahatma Gandhi Institute of Education for Peace and Sustainable Development, the Kabir Project, Shishuvan, Muktangan, and the Prajnya Trust’s Education for Peace Initiative. He can be reached at chintan.writing@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Chintan Girish Modi
“We are all creatures of history,” says former NCERT Director Krishna Kumar, in conversation with Chintan Girish Modi. They discuss the sensitive role of history teachers, the director’s latest work, and the future of history education.

item.title: Where does training help?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Shishu Ranjan

Teacher 1: R

R was appointed as assistant teacher at a primary school in 2014. She had nine years of teaching experience in a government school and two and a half years in a private school in Bengaluru. She did her intermediate in science with biology as her major. She wanted to become a doctor, but couldn’t apply for the AIPMT (All India Pre-Medical Test) as she had crossed the age bar. Therefore, she joined a BA English course and then studied political science for her Masters. She wanted to become a journalist. But marriage, household responsibilities, and kids meant that she couldn’t pursue that dream either. On her grandmother’s suggestion, R did her Bachelor of Education (BEd) from a government BEd college in Uttarakhand.

R belongs to a well-to-do and educated farmer family and has always been supported in her education. Even after her marriage, she received support from her husband and in-laws.

Teacher 2: A

A is the Head Teacher in a government upper primary school. She was appointed an assistant teacher in 1998 in a primary school and then promoted to an upper primary school in 2004. A did her intermediate in science with biology as her major. She has 25 years of experience teaching in the government system. Prior to teaching in a government school, she taught in a private school. She wanted to become a lecturer in a college, but pressure to get married and lack of support forced her to drop out from BSc and pursue the Basic Training Certificate (BTC) for teaching in school instead. A is inspired by her grandparents who always motivated her to study and get a job.

Analysis

I met with these two teachers to understand what they thought about the pre-service teacher training and continuous professional development courses being organized by the governments. I also wanted to know how their non-academic work was affecting their teaching, how pre-service and in-service teacher training plays a role in the development of a teacher, and how this training affect a teacher’s belief system.

Both teachers have gone through rigorous teacher training programmes, which involved regular classes in an institute of education, delivery of lesson plans during their internships, micro teaching, etc. These teachers believed that their teacher training was good for their professional development.

In addition to her B.Ed. course, R attended a 90-day induction programme at the District Institute of Education and Training (DIET), Uttarakhand when she joined the system in 2014. She got to meet and interact with experienced people in the system during that time. The training provided by DIET, according to her, was better than her B.Ed. programme as she got to study different pedagogies and educational theories. She was exposed to activity-based teaching as well during the training.

When I asked her about Continuous Professional Development (CPD), she was quite disappointed with the education department. The CPD programmes were not very regular. She attended only a few meetings and workshops in the past nine years. However, she has been a key resource person for FLN (Foundational Literacy and Numeracy) training in blocks of the districts. She considers the training she received during her induction programme at the DIET (conducted in collaboration with the Azim Premji Foundation) the best training she has received so far. She has been part of the District Resource Group (DRG)since 2015. This group helps teachers and schools that face challenges related to school functioning.

In 2020, during the first COVID-19 lockdown, she connected online with the Azim Premji Foundation (APF). Here, she took part in webinars, online trainings, and discussed and reflected on relevant articles, and developed worksheets for students, which involved cognitive stimulation and resulted in innovative teaching. Her experience with the Foundation led her to becoming a part of a voluntary teachers’ forum guided by the Foundation. Here, she received a lot of respect and support in terms of her teaching skills, something she never got before. She attended a six day social studies seminar organized by APF at the District Institute, Dineshpur, which inspired her to become a Mentor Teacher (MT). APF not only encouraged her to teach innovatively, but also to continue to learn throughout the teaching journey. She considers that the professional development procedure adopted by APF should be replicated by the government system. Cluster Resource Centres/Block Resource Centres should not remain as centres only to distribute resources and collect survey reports, but they should also work like Teaching Learning Centres, where teachers who need it get immediate support.

Her roles as Mentor Teacher and Head Teacher feed back into her own teaching. She says that working with students in primary schools needs a lot of effort and continuity, especially with learners from lower socio-economic backgrounds. When this continuity breaks, it affects the children’s learning. She listed many non-academic tasks, not directly related to classroom teaching, which take up a lot of her time and therefore impact her teaching directly. She feels that a teacher should not be doing anything other than teaching. A teacher, she believes, should be allowed to spend time with her students in the classroom and engage them in academic tasks as much as possible. To improve themselves, teachers must be given time to think. Not every teacher can multitask.

As Nasreen et. Al (2019) said in their paper, non-academic tasks not only affect classroom teaching but also lead to deficient performance in teachers. Multiple reports and recommendations** have been made to reduce the non-academic tasks of teachers.

A had the same opinion on pre-service teacher training since she had attended the BTC training program for two years. When it came to in-service teacher training, A said that it was not adequate in terms of a school and teacher’s needs. She believes that the block level officers and cluster level inspectors should focus more on need-based training for teachers. She gave her own example. When she was promoted to upper primary school as a science teacher in 2005, she was the only teacher in the newly built school. Instead of supporting her, the local level functionaries were more focused on insulting the teachers. She was told that she wouldn’t last long in the school because there were no students in the school. She then started connecting with the parents of the village and the neighbouring village to bring students. Within two months, she got five admissions. In the next admission session, she had 50 students. In her third year, it was 125 and in the 4th 225. Most of these four years, she handled the students alone. Later, she was supported by four more teachers. Despite wanting to teach in the classroom, when she was offered the post of the Head Teacher, she accepted the challenge.

She did not receive any training related to school management, however. She took help from her network of Head Teachers in the block and devised her own ways of running the school. A believes that there is an urgent need for support in dealing with school management issues because the transition from an assistant teacher to head teacher is not easy, as a whole bunch of new tasks are added.

She mentioned that she and her colleagues received basic computer training for 10 days in Dehradun, but she never received any training related to the Education Portal, UDISE, PARAKH, and PFMS data updating. She learned everything by herself from YouTube.

Reflection and conclusion: Both teachers are innovative in many ways, but they have genuine concerns related to the non-academic tasks. Because of exposure and their capabilities, they were able to multiple challenges, but due to their additional responsibilities they are struggling to continue their classroom teaching regularly. They both have a desire to learn and are a source of inspiration for many women in their society.

The aims of the National Curriculum Framework for Teacher Education (NCFTE) 2009 (NCTE, 2009, p.64) suggest that the continuous professional development (CPD) of teachers should allow the teachers to “explore, reflect on and develop one’s own practice”, “deepen one’s knowledge of and update oneself about one’s academic discipline or other areas of school curriculum” and teachers should be able to “research and reflect on learners and their education”. But the experiences of teachers are completely contrary to what the NCFTE suggests because non-academic tasks not only keep the teachers away from their students but also from their preparation to teach and update their knowledge of content.

Most importantly, NCFTE suggests “giving teachers a space to develop and hear their own voices is of utmost importance.” But teachers complain that their voice is invisible to the government.

Educators like Henry Giroux, Paulo Freire, and bell hooks have advocated that teachers should be seen as transformatory intellectuals rather than as mere technicians. It is important to engage teachers in curriculum formation and planning for educational processes and not just its implementation.

*Teachers’ names have been coded for anonymity.

** https://indianexpress.com/article/cities/ahmedabad/primary-teachers-spend-twothird-of-their-time-in-nonteaching-work-amc-board-study/

https://www.ijicc.net/images/Vol_15/Iss_7/15762_Yousaf_2021_E1_R.pdf

(Page-22): https://d1wqtxts1xzle7.cloudfront.net/41798591/Teacher_Motivation_in_India20160131-29787-mfczi2-libre.pdf?1454234768=&response-content-disposition=inline%3B+filename%3DTeacher_Motivation_In_India.pdf&Expires=1745799668&Signature=FdDEpNhw7AdcnVQU2jjvrE85TI4Il~9OA59wy1UE1UTbdIwKcE7UDkxLC2tngM~bwtbodiVMj14gKdsG8I-LPQL13Qe4p~UmgBMPK0~ebnLcwY46CK8LYca4vBPvRV90ijylNmcEJWcdXqx1f21NOeFbV9gBxjPS4PbkIHFvh7TEXOMYxSpGOiKB8nkmBD3y1p5tugLNju40eOYjc-ma9U1OszUNSitZZe35nAUUenTqPEg~sOwOduuvkvQTQvz8Qh0fhd4u96vC9z~3DxCcwJ72lHLTEpmG8xo6cYoP9Yr2CXryZBrzpWUqKObfR9SkJ0dwGm9hOHshoNTwbE~rYA__&Key-Pair-Id=APKAJLOHF5GGSLRBV4ZA

References

  1. 677 Vimala Ramachandran, The misunderstood schoolteacher. (n.d.). Retrieved November 26, 2023, from https://www.indiaseminar.com/2016/677/677_vimala_ramachandran.htm
  2. Feiman-Nemser, S. (2001). From Preparation to Practice: Designing a Continuum to Strengthen and Sustain Teaching. Teachers College Record, 103(6), 1013-1055. https://doi.org/10.1111/0161-4681.00141
  3. Nasreen, A. (2019). Teachers’ Time: Teaching Practices along with Non-academic Additional Duties of Elementary School Teachers. Pakistan Social Sciences Review, 3(I), 409-419. https://doi.org/10.35484/pssr.2019(3-I)30
  4. NCTE. (2009). National Curriculum Framework for Teacher Education 2009. National Council for Teacher Education. https://ncte.gov.in/website/PDF/NCFTE_2009.pdf

Shishu Ranjan is a Chevening Scholar, studying MSc Education and Social Justice at Birkbeck, University of London. He has completed his MA in Education from Azim Premji University and BEd from Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi. He is interested in teacher education and social justice. He can be reached at shishu.ranjan22_mae@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shishu Ranjan
From festival duties to online portals, Shishu Ranjan writes about two committed teachers who push through systemic gaps to teach, lead, and inspire.

item.title: Precision in practice: turning errors into opportunities
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
P. Juliet Nancy

I am a mathematics teacher for grade X in a renowned school. I enrolled for a course on “seeing missed takes in mistakes”. When my facilitator shared the course timeline and listed the follow-up actions (that included my students completing some written assignments and tasks), I was taken aback.

I wondered, “How am I going to do all this with my grade X students? After all, as a teacher I had to complete the syllabus on time. And I could not deviate from the prescribed syllabus. It was grade X after all!”

So, it was with this apprehension that I started the course.

Preparing the ground

I began planning and seeing how I could incorporate the course demands without disturbing the flow of my class, bearing in mind my main intent, which was to see how best I could help my students. I maintained a positive mindset and approach.

I first had to make my students comfortable and create an ‘errors are okay’ environment in the classroom. I did this by sharing my own experiences as a student. It took two to three weeks for my students to accept that they made a mistake in front of the class. The purpose of this initial step was to help students identify the errors they were making and then enable them to overcome these errors by different methods.

Where did I start?

First, I prepared dummy worksheets, focusing on chapters the students were making repetitive errors in. They were asked to

  1. take a look at the dummy worksheets
  2. identify the errors
  3. guess what the child might have been thinking while committing the error. What was missing? Could they come up with the right answers and strategies to arrive at these answers?

I then asked the students to draw a mind map with the common errors that they made. Thereafter, we started working on their individual errors one at a time.

See Figure 1 for dummy worksheet, Table for students’ responses, and Figure 2 for a Mind map of errors.

Figure 1: Dummy worksheet

Table: Students’ responses

*Names of the students have been changed to maintain privacy*

Figure 2: Mind map of errors

I attended fortnightly sessions with my facilitator. We would talk about what I was doing and what I was going to do and she would offer timely suggestions and feedback.

As I was teaching trigonometry, I found that my students were finding it difficult to identify the particular identity that had to be used and get the application of basic principles right.

Therefore, based on my facilitator’s suggestion, I started making level-wise task sheets on trigonometry as below:

Level 1: simple understanding-based

Level 2: more advanced understanding-based and

Level 3: application-based.

Shown below are the three levels of task sheets for trigonometry:

Students were given the choice to select the level and then the sheet was posted in Google Classroom with an option to solve the other levels too.

Since this was available online for all to see, I noticed that peer-learning was happening when students were helping others understand what was necessary and what was missing.

Now it was time for our mid-year assessment.

How far had they absorbed the new learning?

During my revision for the mid-year assessment, I divided the students into groups, where each group crafted questions to ask the other groups. I stressed that those who were giving the questions should also know the solution to the questions they framed. So here, the students donned the role of a teacher and I was just facilitating as and when required.

Now, I noticed that the students were becoming more responsible and taking the onus of the assigned work. I was also able to gauge the kind of questions that they were giving, the skills involved and the difficulty levels. The students’ performance was better this time. It wasn’t as if there were no errors, but the number of errors were fewer, and by regular practice, I was sure that the errors would come down further.

The way forward

As we are now approaching the prelims, I am going ahead with the revision plan of making level-wise task sheets. This will enable me to probe where the students stand and where I should step in to help.

I am glad I was able to do so much despite teaching a board exam class. It was all possible because of proper planning, timely guidance and suggestions from my facilitator, along with proper execution.

Juliet Nancy is Head of Department – Mathematics at Suchitra Academy International School, Hyderabad. She can be reached at julietnancy.p@suchitra.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
P. Juliet Nancy
In a board exam year, P. Juliet Nancy did the unexpected. She devised ways to turn student errors into powerful teaching moments.
